**[RENNERT KRIEGER SOLANO LLP]{.underline}**

**Attorneys at Law** 1185 Avenue of the Americas, 31st Floor New York, New York 10036

**[MEMORANDUM]{.underline}**

**TO:** Brianna Falk, Esq., Whitfield Ames & Trotter LLP (Counsel for Meridian Advanced Materials, LLC)

**FROM:** Tomás Escalante, Esq., Rennert Krieger Solano LLP (Counsel for Kastor Chemical Industries AG)

**DATE:** August 1, 2025

**RE:** Kastor Chemical Industries AG --- Summary of Negotiating Positions on Open JVA Terms (MKPoly JVA v.4, redline dated July 18, 2025)

**PURPOSE:** Third-round negotiation exchange --- position paper for counterparty review. **Not privileged.**

**[I. Introduction and Overview]{.underline}**

This memorandum is transmitted on behalf of our client, Kastor Chemical Industries AG (\"Kastor\"), to Meridian Advanced Materials, LLC (\"Meridian\") and its counsel, Whitfield Ames & Trotter LLP, in anticipation of the principals meeting between Dr. Henrik Laufer, Chief Strategy Officer of Kastor, and Elaine Cordero, Chief Executive Officer of Meridian, currently scheduled for August 11, 2025.

The purpose of this memorandum is to provide a comprehensive summary of Kastor\'s positions, business justifications, minimum acceptable outcomes, and areas of flexibility across the five contested provisions remaining in the Joint Venture Agreement for MKPoly Coatings, LLC (\"MKPoly\" or the \"JV\"), Draft v.4 (redline dated July 18, 2025) (the \"JVA\"). These five provisions --- Management Committee Composition and Voting, Capital Calls, Post-Exit Non-Compete, Exit Valuation Methodology, and IP License-Back and Co-Developed IP Rights --- represent the remaining open terms following two prior rounds of negotiation and exchange.

For each open term, this memorandum states: (i) Kastor\'s preferred outcome and the specific language or structure Kastor seeks; (ii) the business rationale supporting Kastor\'s position; (iii) Kastor\'s minimum acceptable position, below which Kastor is unable to agree to the provision in its current form; and (iv) a cross-reference to the specific section or schedule of the v.4 redline to which the position relates.

This memorandum is **not** marked privileged and is expressly intended for review by Meridian and its counsel to facilitate good-faith resolution of open issues ahead of the target JVA execution date of **August 22, 2025**. Kastor transmits this document in the spirit of transparency and with the objective of narrowing the issues in advance of the principals meeting so that Dr. Laufer and Ms. Cordero may focus their discussion on the remaining areas of genuine disagreement.

We respectfully request that Meridian and its counsel review the positions set forth below and provide a written response, whether in the form of a comparable position paper or a revised redline, in advance of the August 11 meeting. Kastor believes that a candid exchange of positions prior to the principals meeting will significantly enhance the likelihood of reaching final agreement on the JVA within the agreed timeline.

**[II. Kastor\'s Overall Deal Priorities and Areas of Flexibility]{.underline}**

Before addressing each contested provision individually, Kastor believes it is useful to identify its overarching priorities and to signal the areas in which it has the greatest flexibility. Kastor hopes that this transparency will allow Meridian to allocate its own negotiating capital efficiently and to identify trade-offs that produce mutual value.

**A. Core Deal Priorities**

Kastor identifies the following three items as its top-priority concerns, stated in order of importance to Kastor\'s participation in MKPoly. These are the terms on which Kastor has the least room for compromise and which Kastor considers fundamental to the structure of the JV:

**1. Protective Vetoes on Reserved Matters.** Kastor holds a 45% equity interest in MKPoly notwithstanding a total contribution of \$43.8 million --- a figure that is approximately equivalent to, and in fact slightly exceeds, Meridian\'s total contribution of \$42.9 million. The disparity between Kastor\'s economic contribution and its minority equity percentage creates an inherent structural risk that can only be mitigated through governance protections. A supermajority voting requirement on a defined list of Reserved Matters, ensuring that Kastor\'s affirmative consent is required for material corporate actions, is essential to protecting Kastor\'s investment commensurate with its near-parity capital commitment. This protective mechanism is the single most important term to Kastor in the JVA.

**2. Independent Fair-Market-Value Appraisal.** The integrity of the buyout mechanism upon which the exit provisions of the JVA depend is only as strong as the independence of the valuation function. The exit valuation must be conducted by an independent appraiser that is free from conflicts of interest with either party and with MKPoly\'s own financial advisors and auditors. Kastor\'s investment in MKPoly may ultimately be realized through the buyout mechanism, and the fairness of that mechanism requires genuine independence in the appraisal process. This concern is addressed in detail in Section VI below.

**3. Broad Co-Developed IP License-Back.** Kastor\'s contribution of nine items of intellectual property and €38 million (approximately \$41.8 million) in cash directly funds the JV\'s research and development activity. The intellectual property created during the life of the JV will build upon both parties\' contributions and upon JV-funded research. Kastor must retain meaningful rights to co-developed intellectual property upon exit or dissolution of MKPoly. Without such rights, Kastor would be in the untenable position of having funded the creation of valuable intellectual property and then being unable to use the fruits of that investment in its ongoing global chemicals business. A non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to co-developed IP with sublicensing rights is the minimum necessary to preserve the value of Kastor\'s investment.

**B. Areas of Potential Flexibility**

Kastor identifies the following areas in which it has meaningful room to move toward Meridian\'s position. Kastor offers these signals of flexibility in the hope that Meridian will reciprocate with flexibility on Kastor\'s priority items:

**1. Non-Compete Geographic Scope.** Kastor\'s opening position proposes a geographic limitation restricted to territories where MKPoly had active sales at the time of exit. Kastor recognizes that this formulation may be narrower than Meridian considers adequate to protect MKPoly\'s competitive position and goodwill. Kastor is prepared to consider moving toward a broader geographic formulation --- potentially including all of North America --- provided the non-compete scope remains tethered to the JV\'s actual competitive footprint and does not extend to Kastor\'s broader chemical operations outside of the specialty polymer coatings market. Kastor\'s global chemical business encompasses commodity chemicals, industrial solvents, polymer precursors, and numerous other product lines that do not compete with MKPoly, and a geographically unlimited non-compete would unreasonably restrain Kastor\'s ability to operate its diversified business.

**2. Capital Call Cure Period Length.** Kastor\'s opening position of a 90-day cure period is designed to protect against temporary cash-flow timing disruptions arising from Kastor\'s multi-currency treasury operations and Swiss regulatory requirements. Kastor recognizes, however, that Meridian has a legitimate operational need for funding certainty and that an extended cure period can create cash-flow uncertainty for the JV. Kastor is willing to discuss a shortened cure window within a reasonable range --- potentially as short as 60 days --- so long as the dilution consequences of non-funding remain capped and a loan alternative is preserved as an election available to the non-funding member.

**3. EBITDA Multiple for Exit Valuation.** Kastor acknowledges the comparable transactions data compiled by Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP, which shows a range of 6.2× to 8.1× trailing twelve-month EBITDA for transactions in the specialty polymer coatings sector, with a median of 7.1×. Kastor is willing to discuss the specific multiple number within this range and does not take an inflexible position on whether the multiple should be at, above, or below the median. Kastor\'s flexibility on the multiple is, however, contingent upon satisfaction of its appraiser independence requirement (Priority #2 above). The specific number is a secondary concern relative to the process by which the valuation is conducted.

**4. Management Committee / Board Composition.** Kastor identifies board composition --- the number of seats allocated to each party --- as an area of significant flexibility. While Kastor\'s opening proposal of equal representation (three Meridian appointees and three Kastor appointees) reflects the near-parity capital commitment described above, Kastor recognizes that Meridian, as the 55% equity holder and operator of the JV, may seek a board majority. Kastor is prepared to accept asymmetric board composition, including Meridian\'s proposed 3-to-2 structure, **provided that** supermajority voting on Reserved Matters is preserved, ensuring Kastor retains protective veto rights regardless of the number of seats it holds. The composition of the board is of secondary importance to the voting threshold on Reserved Matters.

**[III. Term 1 --- Management Committee Composition and Voting (Section 5.01)]{.underline}**

**A. Kastor\'s Preferred Outcome**

Kastor\'s opening position on Management Committee composition and voting, as reflected in its redline comments to Section 5.01 of v.4, is as follows:

> • **Board composition:** Equal representation of three (3) Meridian-appointed directors and three (3) Kastor-appointed directors, for a total Management Committee of six (6) members.
>
> • **Ordinary voting:** Simple majority for ordinary-course business matters, with the chairperson (to be designated annually on a rotating basis) holding a casting vote in the event of a tie on ordinary-course matters only.
>
> • **Reserved Matters voting:** A defined list of Reserved Matters requiring the affirmative vote of at least five (5) of six (6) directors, which functionally requires the affirmative vote of at least one (1) Kastor-appointed director and at least two (2) Meridian-appointed directors. This constitutes a supermajority threshold that ensures neither party can unilaterally approve a Reserved Matter.

The Reserved Matters list proposed by Kastor encompasses the following categories of material corporate actions:

> \(a\) any amendment to the LLC Agreement or the Certificate of Formation;
>
> \(b\) any change to the stated business purpose of MKPoly as set forth in Schedule 2.04;
>
> \(c\) the admission of any new member or the issuance of additional membership interests;
>
> \(d\) any capital call in excess of \$5,000,000 per fiscal year;
>
> \(e\) the incurrence of indebtedness in excess of \$2,500,000 individually or \$7,500,000 in the aggregate;
>
> \(f\) the disposition, transfer, or encumbrance of any material asset of MKPoly having a value in excess of \$1,000,000;
>
> \(g\) the entry into, amendment of, or termination of any related-party transaction between MKPoly and either member or any affiliate of either member;
>
> \(h\) the appointment or removal of the Chief Executive Officer, Chief Financial Officer, or Chief Technology Officer of MKPoly;
>
> \(i\) the approval of the annual budget and any material deviation therefrom (defined as a deviation of 10% or more from any budgeted line item);
>
> \(j\) the initiation or settlement of any litigation or claim involving an amount in controversy exceeding \$500,000;
>
> \(k\) the dissolution, liquidation, or winding up of MKPoly; and
>
> \(l\) any transaction constituting a change of control of MKPoly.

**B. Business Justification**

Kastor\'s position on Management Committee composition and voting is driven by the fundamental structural feature of this JV: Kastor\'s total contribution of \$43.8 million --- comprising approximately \$41.8 million in cash (the U.S. dollar equivalent of €38 million at the agreed exchange rate) and a supply-agreement value of approximately \$2.0 million --- exceeds Meridian\'s total contribution of \$42.9 million, which comprises \$28.6 million in contributed intellectual property (23 items valued pursuant to the independent valuation conducted by Frazier Whitmore & Co.) and \$14.3 million in cash. Despite contributing approximately \$900,000 more in total value than Meridian, Kastor holds a 45% equity interest to Meridian\'s 55%.

The parties negotiated this equity split for sound commercial reasons, reflecting Meridian\'s role as operator and the strategic value of Meridian\'s contributed intellectual property portfolio. Kastor does not seek to reopen the equity allocation. However, the disparity between economic contribution and equity percentage creates an imperative for governance protections that compensate for the minority equity position. Without supermajority protections, Kastor --- despite its near-parity economic contribution --- would lack any ability to prevent material corporate actions that could adversely affect its investment. This is the core inequity that the Reserved Matters mechanism is designed to address.

A supermajority requirement on Reserved Matters ensures that actions materially affecting Kastor\'s investment cannot be taken without Kastor\'s consent. These actions include changes to the business purpose described in Schedule 2.04 (Business Purpose), which defines the authorized scope of MKPoly\'s activities and forms the foundation for the parties\' mutual understanding of the JV\'s operations. They also include additional capital calls that could dilute Kastor\'s equity position, related-party transactions that could divert JV value to Meridian or its affiliates, disposition of material assets, changes to the LLC agreement itself, admission of new members that could further dilute Kastor\'s position, and incurrence of indebtedness that could encumber MKPoly\'s assets or increase its risk profile beyond the parties\' agreed parameters.

These are precisely the categories of actions in which a minority member\'s protective rights are most critical. In each case, the majority member has an incentive --- or at least the structural capability --- to take action that benefits its position at the expense of the minority member. The supermajority mechanism aligns governance rights with economic contribution and prevents the conversion of a narrow majority equity position into unilateral control over material decisions.

**C. Minimum Acceptable Position**

Kastor recognizes that Meridian, as the 55% equity holder and the party designated to serve as the day-to-day operator of MKPoly, has a legitimate interest in a board composition that reflects its majority equity position. Kastor is therefore prepared to accept Meridian\'s proposed board composition of **three (3) Meridian-appointed directors and two (2) Kastor-appointed directors**, for a total Management Committee of five (5) members, subject to the following conditions:

> \(i\) The Reserved Matters list set forth above (or a substantially similar list to be negotiated in good faith) is incorporated into Section 5.01, and all Reserved Matters require the affirmative vote of at least **four (4) of five (5)** directors --- i.e., a supermajority threshold requiring the affirmative vote of at least one (1) Kastor-appointed director. This is the functional equivalent of the supermajority requirement in Kastor\'s preferred outcome and ensures that neither party can unilaterally approve a Reserved Matter.
>
> \(ii\) The CEO\'s casting vote on deadlock, which Meridian has proposed in its redline, applies **only** to ordinary-course operational matters and does **not** extend to any Reserved Matter. Because the CEO of MKPoly will be a Meridian appointee, a CEO casting vote on Reserved Matters would render the supermajority requirement entirely illusory and would effectively grant Meridian unilateral decision-making authority over the very matters that the supermajority threshold is designed to protect.

To be clear, the number of board seats is secondary to the veto mechanism. Kastor is willing to move from its preferred equal-representation structure to a 3-to-2 structure in Meridian\'s favor, provided the supermajority voting threshold on Reserved Matters is preserved in full. This represents a meaningful concession from Kastor and is offered in the spirit of reaching agreement.

**D. Deadlock Resolution**

Kastor\'s position on deadlock resolution is as follows. In the event that the Management Committee is unable to reach the required supermajority threshold on a Reserved Matter after good-faith deliberation, the parties should follow a structured resolution process:

> **Step 1 --- Escalation to Principals.** The matter is escalated to the Chief Strategy Officer of Kastor and the Chief Executive Officer of Meridian for direct negotiation within thirty (30) days.
>
> **Step 2 --- Mediation.** If the principals are unable to resolve the deadlock within the thirty-day escalation period, the parties submit the matter to non-binding mediation before a mediator mutually selected by the parties (or, in the absence of agreement, appointed by the American Arbitration Association) within sixty (60) days.
>
> **Step 3 --- Forced Buyout.** If mediation is unsuccessful, either party may initiate a forced buyout of the other party\'s interest at fair market value as determined by an independent appraiser selected through the mechanism described in Section VI below. The party initiating the forced buyout shall have the first right to purchase the other party\'s interest; if neither party elects to purchase, the JV shall be dissolved and wound up in accordance with Section 11.05.

Kastor opposes a CEO casting-vote mechanism on Reserved Matters as a deadlock resolution tool. As noted above, the CEO of MKPoly will be a Meridian appointee. Granting the CEO a casting vote on Reserved Matters --- i.e., on the very matters designated as requiring both parties\' consent --- would effectively convert the supermajority requirement into a Meridian-controlled majority vote. This would deprive Kastor of the protective rights that are the central purpose of the Reserved Matters mechanism and would be inconsistent with the governance balance that the parties have sought to establish. The structured resolution process described above provides a fair and workable alternative that preserves both parties\' rights while ensuring that deadlocks are ultimately resolved.

**[IV. Term 2 --- Capital Calls: Cure Period, Dilution, and Loan Alternative (Section 4.03)]{.underline}**

**A. Kastor\'s Preferred Outcome**

Kastor\'s opening position on capital call mechanics, as reflected in its redline comments to Section 4.03 of v.4, is as follows:

> • **Cure period:** Ninety (90) calendar days from the date of the capital call notice to fund the called amount, before any dilution or other remedy takes effect.
>
> • **Dilution cap:** In the event a member fails to fund a capital call within the cure period, dilution of the non-funding member\'s equity interest is capped at ten percent (10%) of the non-funding member\'s then-current equity interest per annum. Dilution accrues on a pro-rata basis over the course of the fiscal year and does not take effect in a single step.
>
> • **Loan alternative:** The funding member has the right, but not the obligation, to advance the non-funding member\'s share of the capital call as a member loan to MKPoly on behalf of the non-funding member. The loan bears interest at a market rate (defined as the Secured Overnight Financing Rate plus 250 basis points, adjusted quarterly) and is repayable from the non-funding member\'s share of distributions. If the funding member elects to advance the loan, no dilution occurs, and the non-funding member\'s equity percentage is preserved. The non-funding member may elect the loan alternative at any time during the cure period by providing written notice to the funding member and to MKPoly.

**B. Business Justification**

Kastor is a Swiss industrial conglomerate headquartered in the Canton of Zug, Switzerland (Swiss UID CHE-215.847.603), with complex multi-currency treasury operations spanning more than twenty jurisdictions. Capital calls denominated in United States dollars require internal approval through Kastor\'s multi-level treasury authorization process, foreign exchange execution to convert euro-denominated reserves to U.S. dollars, and compliance with Swiss regulatory reporting obligations applicable to cross-border capital transfers. These are not discretionary delays; they are inherent features of operating a global treasury within a regulated Swiss corporate structure.

Temporary cash-flow timing constraints should not permanently destroy a member\'s equity position. A 90-day cure period and a 10% per annum dilution cap reflect the commercial reality that short-term liquidity disruptions --- arising from treasury processing timelines, foreign exchange settlement cycles, or temporary mismatches between inflows and outflows --- are not indicative of an inability or unwillingness to fund the JV\'s capital needs. They are logistical in nature and are fully consistent with a member\'s good-faith commitment to fund its pro-rata share of capital calls.

The loan alternative is a critical component of the capital-call mechanism because it aligns the interests of both parties. If the funding member advances the non-funding member\'s share as a loan, MKPoly receives immediate access to the required capital --- the operational need is satisfied without delay. The non-funding member\'s equity position is preserved, and the funding member earns a market-rate return on the advance. Both parties benefit: the funding member is compensated for the time value of its advance, and the non-funding member avoids a permanent and potentially disproportionate loss of equity triggered by a matter of days or weeks.

By contrast, Meridian\'s proposed formulation --- a 30-day cure period with uncapped immediate pro-rata dilution and no loan alternative --- could result in a dramatic and permanent transfer of equity value triggered by a short-term timing mismatch that bears no relationship to the non-funding member\'s financial capacity or willingness to fund. Under Meridian\'s proposal, a member that is fully able and willing to fund a capital call but requires 45 days rather than 30 days to process a multi-million-dollar cross-border transfer would suffer immediate, uncapped dilution of its equity interest. In a JV where both members have committed roughly equivalent total capital, this outcome is disproportionate to the harm (a brief delay in funding) and is inconsistent with the long-term, collaborative nature of the JV relationship that both parties seek to establish.

Kastor further notes that the loan alternative creates a natural incentive for the non-funding member to cure promptly. The market-rate interest accruing on the loan provides the non-funding member with a strong financial incentive to repay the advance as quickly as possible, while ensuring that the funding member is made whole for the time value of its capital. This is a more balanced and efficient mechanism than punitive, uncapped dilution.

**C. Minimum Acceptable Position**

Kastor would accept a cure period shorter than 90 days, **provided it is not shorter than sixty (60) calendar days** and the following protections are maintained in full:

> \(i\) **Dilution cap:** Dilution for non-funding is capped at no more than ten percent (10%) of the non-funding member\'s then-current equity interest per annum. This cap ensures that dilution consequences are proportionate to the harm caused by non-funding and prevents a single missed capital call from triggering a wholesale transfer of equity value.
>
> \(ii\) **Loan alternative:** A loan alternative at a market-rate interest (SOFR plus 250 basis points, or such other market-rate formulation as the parties may agree) remains available to the non-funding member as an election that may be made at any time during the cure period, prior to dilution taking effect. If the loan alternative is elected, no dilution occurs.

Kastor will not accept uncapped immediate dilution without a loan alternative. This is a firm position. A capital-call mechanism that imposes uncapped, immediate dilution with no loan alternative and a cure period shorter than 60 days is, in Kastor\'s view, inconsistent with the fundamental character of a long-term joint venture between sophisticated commercial parties and creates a risk of opportunistic capital calls designed to dilute the minority member rather than to fund legitimate operational needs.

**[V. Term 3 --- Post-Exit Non-Compete (Section 11.01)]{.underline}**

**A. Kastor\'s Preferred Outcome**

Kastor\'s opening position on the post-exit non-compete, as reflected in its redline comments to Section 11.01 of v.4, is as follows:

> • **Duration:** One (1) year from the effective date of the exiting member\'s exit or the dissolution of MKPoly, whichever occurs first.
>
> • **Product scope:** Limited to the specific JV product lines as defined in Schedule 2.04 (Business Purpose) at the time of exit or dissolution. The restriction does not extend to \"all specialty polymer coatings\" or to any product line that is not within the JV\'s defined scope of operations.
>
> • **Geographic scope:** Limited to the territories in which MKPoly had active sales --- defined as territories in which MKPoly generated revenue from product sales or licensing activities --- during the twelve (12) months preceding the exit or dissolution event.

**B. Business Justification**

Kastor\'s broader chemical portfolio is substantially more diversified than MKPoly\'s operations. Kastor manufactures and distributes commodity chemicals, industrial solvents, polymer precursors, specialty additives, and numerous other product lines across more than forty countries. The vast majority of these product lines are not coatings, are not within the scope of MKPoly\'s business purpose as defined in Schedule 2.04, and do not compete with MKPoly in any market.

A non-compete defined by reference to \"all specialty polymer coatings\" --- as Meridian has proposed --- would capture a substantial portion of Kastor\'s existing global business that falls entirely outside the scope of the JV\'s operations. By way of example, Kastor\'s industrial protective coatings division, which produces anti-corrosion coatings for maritime and infrastructure applications, could be characterized as within the broad category of \"specialty polymer coatings\" but has no competitive overlap with MKPoly\'s consumer and automotive coatings product lines. A non-compete of this breadth would operate as a restraint on Kastor\'s pre-existing business operations, not merely as a protection of MKPoly\'s goodwill and competitive position.

The non-compete must be narrowly tailored to protect MKPoly\'s legitimate competitive interests --- the specific product lines developed and commercialized by the JV, in the specific markets in which the JV operates --- without unreasonably restraining Kastor\'s ability to operate the remainder of its diversified global chemicals business. This is not only a matter of commercial fairness but also a matter of enforceability. Delaware courts and Texas courts (the two jurisdictions most likely to adjudicate disputes under the JVA) enforce post-exit non-competes in the commercial context, but both jurisdictions apply reasonableness standards that require a non-compete to be tailored in scope, geography, and duration to the legitimate protectable interest. Overbroad non-competes are routinely narrowed or struck entirely.

With respect to duration, a one-year period is within the safe harbor recognized by courts in both jurisdictions for commercial non-competes. Delaware courts have upheld non-competes of one to two years in the LLC and joint-venture context; a one-year period is adequate to protect MKPoly\'s goodwill and customer relationships during the transition following a member\'s exit. With respect to geography, limitation to MKPoly\'s actual sales territories avoids the overbreadth that could render the provision unenforceable and focuses the restriction on the markets in which MKPoly has a genuine competitive interest to protect.

**C. Minimum Acceptable Position**

Kastor would accept a non-compete duration of up to **eighteen (18) months** --- representing a significant movement from its preferred one-year position --- subject to the following conditions:

> \(i\) **Product scope** is limited to the specific JV product lines identified in Schedule 2.04 (Business Purpose) and does not extend to \"all specialty polymer coatings\" or to any product category outside the JV\'s defined scope of operations. Kastor is willing to work with Meridian to develop a detailed product-line description for incorporation into Schedule 2.04 that provides Meridian with adequate protection while preserving Kastor\'s ability to operate its non-competing product lines.
>
> \(ii\) **Geographic scope** is limited to **North America** --- defined as the United States, Canada, and Mexico --- which constitutes MKPoly\'s operational territory. Kastor would consider accepting \"North America\" as the geographic scope, which is broader than its preferred formulation of MKPoly\'s actual sales territories, in exchange for the narrower product-scope definition described in clause (i) above.

Kastor will **not** accept a combination of broad product scope (\"all specialty polymer coatings\"), broad geography (North America or global), and a duration exceeding eighteen (18) months. Such a combination would be unreasonably restrictive, would extend well beyond the protection of MKPoly\'s legitimate competitive interests, and would create significant enforceability risk under applicable law.

Kastor further notes that Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors) may serve as a useful mechanism to provide additional specificity to the non-compete. If the parties are able to agree on a Restricted Competitors list that identifies the specific entities against which the non-compete is intended to operate, this list could supplement the product-scope and geographic-scope limitations and provide both parties with greater certainty regarding the restriction\'s reach.

**[VI. Term 4 --- Exit Valuation Methodology (Sections 10.03--10.04)]{.underline}**

**A. Kastor\'s Preferred Outcome**

Kastor\'s opening position on exit valuation methodology, as reflected in its redline comments to Sections 10.03 and 10.04 of v.4, is as follows:

> • **Valuation standard:** Fair market value of MKPoly, determined on a going-concern basis as of the date of the trigger event.
>
> • **Appraiser:** A jointly selected independent Big Four accounting firm (i.e., Deloitte, EY, KPMG, or PricewaterhouseCoopers) that has no pre-existing engagement with MKPoly, Kastor, or Meridian or their respective affiliates.
>
> • **Valuation methodology:** Trailing twelve-month EBITDA multiple, with the specific multiple determined by the appraiser based on comparable transactions in the specialty polymer coatings sector.
>
> • **Appraiser selection mechanism:** Each party selects one qualified appraiser. Those two appraisers jointly select a third appraiser, who serves as the final valuation authority. The final valuation is conducted through baseball arbitration: each party submits a proposed valuation figure to the third appraiser, and the appraiser selects the figure that is closer to the appraiser\'s own independent determination of fair market value. This mechanism incentivizes both parties to submit reasonable figures.

**B. Business Justification --- Independence Concern**

Meridian has proposed that MKPoly\'s own auditor, Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP, serve as the valuation appraiser in the event of a buyout triggered by any of the JVA\'s exit provisions. Kastor raises a fundamental independence concern with this proposal.

An entity that serves as the JV\'s auditor maintains an ongoing commercial relationship with MKPoly. That commercial relationship creates financial incentives --- both direct (audit fees) and indirect (relationship-maintenance considerations) --- that are inconsistent with the independence required of a valuation appraiser in a buyout scenario. Moreover, because MKPoly\'s board is controlled by Meridian appointees (whether under a 3-to-3 or 3-to-2 structure, Meridian appoints the CEO), the JV\'s auditor has a primary reporting relationship to a Meridian-controlled management structure. In a buyout scenario --- particularly a forced buyout triggered by deadlock or change of control, where the interests of the two members are directly adverse --- the auditor\'s dual role as both ongoing service provider to MKPoly and valuation appraiser creates an inherent and irreconcilable conflict of interest.

This concern is not academic. The valuation determined by the appraiser in a buyout scenario directly determines the price at which one member acquires the other\'s interest. A difference of even 0.5× on the EBITDA multiple, applied to MKPoly\'s projected EBITDA, could translate into millions of dollars in purchase-price difference. The stakes are too high, and the potential for actual or perceived conflict is too great, to entrust the valuation function to an entity with an ongoing commercial relationship with the JV and its controlling member.

Kastor\'s proposal for a Big Four firm selected through the neutral, three-appraiser baseball-arbitration mechanism described above eliminates this conflict entirely. The final appraiser is selected not by either party but by two independent appraisers, each selected by one party. The baseball-arbitration format incentivizes both parties to submit reasonable valuation figures, because the appraiser selects only one figure --- the one closer to the appraiser\'s own independent determination. This mechanism produces fair, market-based outcomes and avoids both actual bias and the appearance of bias.

Kastor acknowledges that Frazier Whitmore & Co.\'s comparable transactions data --- reflecting a range of 6.2× to 8.1× trailing twelve-month EBITDA for specialty polymer coatings transactions, with a median of 7.1× --- is a useful and well-researched data set. Kastor does not object to the data itself, and Kastor anticipates that any competent independent appraiser would consider this data (among other sources) in conducting a valuation analysis. Kastor\'s objection is limited and specific: **the entity that prepared the comparable transactions data and that serves as MKPoly\'s auditor should not also serve as the valuation appraiser in a buyout of a member\'s interest.**

**C. EBITDA Multiple**

Kastor is flexible on the specific EBITDA multiple number and does not take an inflexible position on this point. Kastor\'s preferred approach is for the multiple to be determined by the independent appraiser based on market conditions at the time of the trigger event, which would produce the most accurate and fair reflection of MKPoly\'s value at the relevant date.

However, if the parties prefer to agree on a fixed contractual multiple --- which Kastor understands Meridian has proposed --- Kastor would accept a figure within the range established by the Frazier Whitmore comparable transactions data. Kastor acknowledges the median of 7.1× and is willing to discuss specific numbers within the **6.5× to 7.5× band**. Kastor notes that the appropriate multiple may depend on MKPoly\'s performance at the time of the trigger event and that a fixed multiple necessarily involves an element of compromise on both sides. Kastor is prepared to engage constructively on this point.

**D. Trigger Events**

Kastor\'s proposed trigger events for the buyout mechanism include the following:

> \(a\) **Change of control** of either member;
>
> \(b\) **Material breach** of the JVA by either member, following notice and cure;
>
> \(c\) **Deadlock** on a Reserved Matter, following exhaustion of the escalation and mediation process described in Section III.D above; and
>
> \(d\) **Involuntary transfer** of a member\'s interest, including transfers resulting from bankruptcy, insolvency, receivership, or court-ordered disposition.

Kastor understands from prior exchanges that Meridian\'s proposal limits the trigger events to change of control and material breach only. Kastor considers deadlock and involuntary transfer to be essential additions for the following reasons:

With respect to **deadlock**, the deadlock resolution mechanism described in Section III.D above must have a terminal step. If the principals escalation fails and mediation is unsuccessful, the parties face indefinite paralysis of the JV on a Reserved Matter. Without a buyout trigger at the end of the deadlock resolution process, the JV would be unable to act on the Reserved Matter and would be unable to resolve the impasse. This is a recipe for value destruction. The forced-buyout mechanism provides a fair and definitive resolution that allows one party to acquire the other\'s interest at fair market value and either continue or wind down the JV\'s operations.

With respect to **involuntary transfer**, the JV is premised on a specific relationship between two identified parties. If one member\'s interest is transferred involuntarily --- whether through bankruptcy proceedings, a court-ordered sale, or other compulsory disposition --- the character of the counterparty relationship is fundamentally altered. The non-affected party has a legitimate interest in the ability to exit the JV (or to acquire the affected party\'s interest) in these circumstances, rather than being compelled to continue operating a joint venture with an unknown and unchosen counterparty. Including involuntary transfer as a trigger event protects both parties against this risk.

**E. Minimum Acceptable Position**

Kastor requires that the valuation appraiser be an independent firm that is **not Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP** and that has **no pre-existing engagement** with MKPoly or with either party or their respective affiliates. This is a firm position and reflects Kastor\'s second-highest deal priority, as described in Section II.A above.

With respect to the appraiser selection mechanism, Kastor recognizes that the three-appraiser baseball-arbitration process it has proposed may be more complex than necessary. Kastor would consider a simplified selection mechanism as an alternative, such as the following:

> The parties shall use good-faith efforts to mutually agree on a single Big Four accounting firm (or other nationally recognized independent valuation firm) to serve as the valuation appraiser within thirty (30) days of the trigger event. If the parties are unable to agree within such thirty-day period, the valuation appraiser shall be appointed by the American Arbitration Association upon application by either party.

Kastor is open to other procedural formulations, provided the core independence requirement is maintained. Kastor will **not** accept Frazier Whitmore & Co. as the valuation appraiser under any formulation, whether as sole appraiser, as one of multiple appraisers, or in any other capacity that would give Frazier Whitmore a determinative role in the valuation outcome.

**[VII. Term 5 --- IP License-Back and Co-Developed IP Rights (Section 12.05; Schedules 12.01, 12.02)]{.underline}**

**A. Kastor\'s Preferred Outcome**

Kastor\'s opening position on IP license-back and co-developed IP rights, as reflected in its redline comments to Section 12.05 and Schedules 12.01 and 12.02 of v.4, is as follows:

> • **Co-developed IP (Schedule 12.02):** Upon exit of a member or dissolution of MKPoly, each party receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to all co-developed intellectual property (as defined in Schedule 12.02), with the right to sublicense within its respective territory. \"Respective territory\" is defined as (a) North America for Meridian and (b) all territories outside North America for Kastor, with both parties retaining the right to use co-developed IP in their own operations globally.
>
> • **Contributed IP (Schedule 12.01):** Contributed intellectual property reverts to the contributing party upon exit or dissolution. The non-contributing party receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to use the contributed IP in its own operations, but without sublicensing rights.

**B. Business Justification --- Co-Developed IP**

As identified in Section II.A above, broad co-developed IP license-back is one of Kastor\'s three top-priority items and a core deal priority. The rationale for this position is grounded in the economics of Kastor\'s investment in MKPoly.

Kastor\'s total contribution to MKPoly is \$43.8 million, of which approximately \$41.8 million is in cash. This cash contribution directly funds MKPoly\'s research and development activities, including the salaries and costs of MKPoly\'s R&D team, the facilities and equipment used in R&D, and the direct costs of developing new intellectual property. Co-developed IP created during the JV\'s life will necessarily build on both parties\' contributed IP --- Meridian\'s 23 contributed items and Kastor\'s 9 contributed items --- and on research conducted by MKPoly\'s personnel using JV resources funded by both parties\' capital contributions.

Kastor cannot accept an outcome in which it contributes \$43.8 million to the JV, a substantial portion of which directly funds the creation of new intellectual property, and then exits the JV without meaningful rights to use the fruits of that investment. Such an outcome would mean that Kastor funded the creation of valuable IP but received no lasting benefit from it upon exit --- a result that would be fundamentally inconsistent with the bargained-for exchange underlying the JVA.

A non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to co-developed IP with territorial sublicensing rights is the minimum necessary to preserve the value of Kastor\'s investment upon exit. The license is non-exclusive, meaning Meridian retains full rights to use the same co-developed IP in its own business. It is royalty-free, reflecting the fact that Kastor has already paid for the IP\'s development through its capital contributions. And it is perpetual, reflecting the reality that intellectual property has an indefinite useful life and that Kastor\'s need to use the IP in its business does not terminate at a fixed date.

Both parties have flagged a gap in the current v.4 draft regarding **improvements to contributed IP**. During the life of the JV, MKPoly\'s R&D activity is likely to produce improvements to the intellectual property originally contributed by each party. For example, MKPoly researchers may develop an improved formulation based on one of Meridian\'s 23 contributed patent families, or may optimize a manufacturing process based on one of Kastor\'s 9 contributed process technologies. The current draft does not clearly address whether such improvements are classified as \"co-developed IP\" (Schedule 12.02) or as extensions of the original \"contributed IP\" (Schedule 12.01), and does not specify the license-back rights applicable to improvements.

Kastor proposes that improvements to contributed IP --- defined as any enhancement, modification, derivative work, or adaptation of a party\'s contributed IP that is developed by or on behalf of MKPoly during the term of the JV --- be treated as co-developed IP for purposes of the license-back provisions. This classification ensures that both parties can use improved versions of the IP they originally contributed, which is the commercially logical outcome. A party that contributes IP to the JV should not find, upon exit, that an improved version of its own contributed IP is available only to the other party. Kastor invites Meridian\'s input on appropriate definitions and is prepared to work collaboratively on this issue.

**C. Sublicensing Rights**

Kastor\'s position is that both parties should have sublicensing rights with respect to co-developed IP within their respective territories. This is not a novel or unusual provision; sublicensing rights are standard in technology joint ventures and reflect the commercial reality that exploiting intellectual property in a global market frequently requires collaboration with manufacturing partners, distributors, and licensees.

Kastor operates globally and requires the ability to sublicense co-developed IP to manufacturing partners and distributors in markets outside North America. Without sublicensing rights, Kastor\'s license to co-developed IP would be effectively limited to products manufactured and sold directly by Kastor --- a significant constraint on the commercial value of the license and one that is not justified by any legitimate interest of Meridian or MKPoly.

Kastor acknowledges Meridian\'s concern, expressed in prior exchanges, regarding sublicensing to North American competitors. Kastor takes this concern seriously and is prepared to address it directly. Kastor proposes the following framework:

> \(a\) Both parties have sublicensing rights within their respective territories (North America for Meridian; all territories outside North America for Kastor).
>
> \(b\) Neither party may sublicense co-developed IP to any entity listed on a mutually agreed Restricted Competitors schedule (to be set forth in Schedule 12.05). The Restricted Competitors schedule would identify specific entities --- by name --- to which neither party may sublicense co-developed IP.
>
> \(c\) The Restricted Competitors schedule is updated annually by mutual agreement of the parties.
>
> \(d\) The sublicensing restriction on Restricted Competitors applies **reciprocally** --- Meridian is equally restricted from sublicensing to entities on the Restricted Competitors list. This reciprocity ensures fairness and prevents the restriction from operating as a one-sided constraint on Kastor\'s business.
>
> \(e\) The Restricted Competitors restriction does not extend to all sublicensing globally. A blanket prohibition on sublicensing would render the license effectively non-transferable and would substantially diminish its commercial value.

**D. Minimum Acceptable Position**

Kastor requires, at minimum, the following terms with respect to IP license-back and co-developed IP rights:

> \(i\) A **non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual** license to all co-developed IP (as defined in Schedule 12.02) upon exit or dissolution. This license must be available to **both parties** on equivalent terms --- Kastor will not accept a structure in which only Meridian receives a license to co-developed IP.
>
> \(ii\) **Sublicensing rights outside North America.** Kastor requires the ability to sublicense co-developed IP to manufacturing partners and distributors in markets outside of North America. Kastor would accept a prohibition on sublicensing to entities listed on a mutually agreed Restricted Competitors schedule (Schedule 12.05), provided (a) the restriction is reciprocal, (b) the schedule is limited to specifically identified entities and not to broad categories of potential competitors, and (c) the schedule is updated annually by mutual agreement.
>
> \(iii\) **Contributed IP reversion** to the contributing party upon exit or dissolution. Kastor understands that this point is agreed by both sides and is not in dispute.

Kastor will **not** accept a structure in which only Meridian receives a license to co-developed IP upon exit or dissolution. Such a structure would mean that Kastor funds the creation of intellectual property and then walks away with no rights to use it --- an outcome that is fundamentally at odds with the economics of Kastor\'s participation in the JV. Kastor similarly will not accept a structure in which Kastor receives no sublicensing rights whatsoever, as this would render the license commercially unworkable for a global company that routinely operates through manufacturing and distribution partners.

  Exit Valuation Methodology                                   §§ 10.03--10.04              Independent Big Four appraiser; baseball arbitration selection; market-determined EBITDA multiple                                   Independent appraiser (not Frazier Whitmore); simplified selection (mutual agreement / AAA fallback); multiple within 6.5×--7.5× range                                      Frazier Whitmore & Co. as appraiser; 6.5× EBITDA fixed multiple                                                                  **High** (on multiple); **Low** (on appraiser independence)

  IP License-Back and Co-Developed IP Rights                   § 12.05; Sch. 12.01, 12.02   Non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to both parties; territorial sublicensing; improvements treated as co-developed IP   Non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to both parties; sublicensing outside N.A.; Restricted Competitors carve-out (Sch. 12.05); contributed IP reversion agreed   Exclusive royalty-free perpetual license to Meridian only; no Kastor license to co-developed IP; no Kastor sublicensing rights   **Low**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[IX. Concluding Remarks]{.underline}**

Kastor is committed to closing the MKPoly Joint Venture Agreement by the target execution date of **August 22, 2025**, and to achieving an operational launch of MKPoly Coatings, LLC in **Q1 2026** as contemplated by the parties\' letter of intent. Kastor views the formation of MKPoly as a significant strategic opportunity that leverages the complementary strengths of both parties --- Meridian\'s market-leading intellectual property portfolio and North American commercial platform, and Kastor\'s global manufacturing capabilities, raw-material supply chain, and capital resources --- to create a specialty polymer coatings enterprise with substantial competitive advantages.

Kastor has structured the positions set forth in this memorandum to protect its \$43.8 million investment and to ensure that the governance, economic, and intellectual-property terms of the JVA reflect the near-parity of the parties\' contributions, while maintaining the flexibility necessary to reach agreement with Meridian on terms that serve both parties\' long-term interests. As detailed above, Kastor has identified meaningful areas of flexibility on board composition, cure period duration, non-compete geography, and the specific EBITDA multiple --- and has stated clearly the terms on which it cannot compromise, including the supermajority veto on Reserved Matters, appraiser independence, and co-developed IP license-back rights.

Kastor looks forward to productive discussions between Dr. Henrik Laufer and Ms. Elaine Cordero at the principals meeting scheduled for **August 11, 2025**, and respectfully invites Meridian and its counsel to respond to these positions --- whether by written position paper, revised redline, or other form --- in advance of that date, so that the principals meeting may focus on the narrowest possible set of remaining issues.

Mr. Escalante is available to discuss any aspect of this memorandum with Ms. Falk at her convenience. Please do not hesitate to reach out.

> Fax: (212) 554-8101

*Top-Priority Items for Kastor:*

ntees. However, we identify this as an area of greater flexibility. Given Mer=

% equity interest (total contribution of $43.8M, comprising =E2=82=AC38M cash=

ognizes that Meridian may have legitimate reasons for seeking board compositi=

**EBITDA Multiple Number.** As noted above, Kastor has flexibility on the spe=

**Requested Deliverable Format**

I have two key requests regarding the format of the compromise document, both=

First, I would ask that the draft include actual proposed compromise contract=

Second, Dr. Laufer has specifically requested that the compromise document be=

 This means each of the five contested terms should be presented as a self-co=
ntained section containing: (a) the proposed compromise clause language; (b) =

For clarity, the five contested terms to be addressed are: (1) management com=
mittee/governance (Section 5.01); (2) capital calls/failure to fund (Section =
4.03); (3) non-compete (Section 11.01); (4) exit valuation (Sections 10.03=E2=
=80=9310.04); and (5) IP license-back (Section 12.05 and Schedules 12.01, 12.=
02, 12.05). The balance of concessions across all five terms should reflect b=
oth sides' priority rankings =E2=80=94 concessions should be larger on lower-=
priority items and more carefully calibrated on top-priority items for each s=

**Specific Points to Flag for Compromise Drafting**

I want to flag a few specific items for your attention as you approach the co=

mise should offer a credible middle path. Please note that Kastor's supermajo=
rity/Reserved Matters position may interact with the deadlock mechanism =E2=

ridian's is 30 days. There is room for compromise in the range of approximate=

ld be available rather than only immediate dilution upon failure to fund. Ple=

sition of uncapped immediate pro-rata dilution =E2=80=94 this is an important=
 structural distinction.

- *Non-compete scope and geography (Section 11.01):* Beyond duration, the com=
promise must address scope (Kastor: only specific JV product lines at exit; M=

ions may apply and should be addressed in the drafting rationale.

osition is sublicensing by both parties in their respective territories. We a=

perhaps through a restricted competitors schedule or a territorial carve-out.=
 Both sides also need a workable framework for improvements and co-developed =

fer as trigger events; Meridian's includes only change of control and materia=

**Timeline**

I would respectfully request that you circulate the draft compromise document=

rincipals meeting. On our end, I commit to providing prompt feedback once the=

If it would be useful to discuss the drafting approach on any of the five ter=
ms in the interim, I am happy to schedule a call at your convenience. Our sha=
red objective remains JVA execution by August 22, entity formation by Septemb=
er 2, and Q1 2026 operational launch =E2=80=94 and I believe this process pos=

Thank you again for your collaborative approach to this, Brianna. I am confid=
ent that a well-structured compromise document will allow Dr. Laufer and Ms. =

**2. Management Committee / Board Composition =E2=80=94 Important**

Our position is 3-2 board composition reflecting our 55/45 equity split. Kast=
or wants 3-3 =E2=80=94 equal representation despite unequal equity. For conte=
xt: our total contribution is $42.9M ($28.6M IP + $14.3M cash); Kastor's is $=
43.8M ($41.8M cash at the =E2=82=AC38M conversion plus $2.0M supply agreement=

The 3-2 structure is important to me, but I recognize Kastor may need somethi=
ng in return. I can accept a Kastor veto on enumerated Reserved Matters requi=
ring supermajority or unanimous consent =E2=80=94 if the list is kept narrow.=

bers as an area of greater flexibility on their side. They may concede 3-2 if=

imited to the genuinely existential items: capital structure changes, related=

rced buyout. I want Meridian's majority-appointed member to retain some form =
of tie-breaking mechanism on operational (non-Reserved) matters. On Reserved =
Matters, I understand deadlock resolution needs to be more balanced =E2=80=94=
 explore a compromise structure, but don't give away operational tie-breaking=
 entirely.

**3. Exit Valuation / EBITDA Multiple =E2=80=94 Flexible**

Our opening position has been 6.5=C3=97 trailing 12-month EBITDA. Kastor has =
been pushing for a higher multiple and independent valuation. The Frazier Whi=
tmore comparable transactions analysis shows a range of 6.2=C3=97=E2=80=938.1=
=C3=97 with a median of 7.1=C3=97.

I'm comfortable moving to 7.0=C3=97. That's a meaningful concession =E2=80=94=
 we're essentially meeting Kastor near the market median, supported by indepe=
ndent data. The Frazier Whitmore analysis gives us solid ground to stand on.

On appraiser selection: our position has been the JV's own auditor (Frazier W=
hitmore). Kastor proposes a jointly-selected independent Big Four firm, with =
each side picking one appraiser, those two selecting a third, and baseball ar=
bitration. I understand Kastor's independence concern =E2=80=94 the auditor a=
rguably shouldn't also serve as valuation appraiser. I'm open to discussing a=
ppraiser selection mechanics, but see if you can propose something simpler th=
an the three-appraiser baseball arbitration. A single jointly-selected indepe=
ndent firm, perhaps with a fallback selection mechanism if we can't agree, wo=
uld be preferable.

On trigger events: we've proposed change of control and material breach. Kast=
or wants those plus deadlock and involuntary transfer. I'll leave this to you=
r legal judgment, but I'm open to including deadlock and involuntary transfer=
 as triggers if the valuation mechanics are fair and the 7.0=C3=97 multiple (=
or a reasonable independent process) governs.

Reference Sections 10.03=E2=80=9310.04.

**4. Non-Compete =E2=80=94 Acceptable at Compromise**

Our opening: 2-year non-compete covering all specialty polymer coatings in No=
rth America. Kastor's opening: 1 year, limited to specific JV product lines, =
limited to territories where the JV had active sales at exit.

I can accept 18 months. That sits comfortably within the Delaware and Texas e=
nforceability safe harbor of 1=E2=80=932 years in commercial contexts, and si=
nce MKPoly is a Delaware LLC with operations HQ in Houston, we're on solid gr=
ound. (I know California generally prohibits non-competes but has LLC/partner=

The real fight here is scope. Kastor's "specific JV product lines" is too nar=
row =E2=80=94 it invites them to develop adjacent products the day after exit=

I can extend the cure period to approximately 75 days if dilution mechanics r=

**Next Steps**

I need the draft compromise provisions delivered to Escalante by August 6. Ea=

 IP license-back (12.05) =E2=80=94 should be presented as a standalone provis=

ules 2.04 (Business Purpose), 12.01 (Contributed IP), 12.02 (Co-Developed IP)=

**[I. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY & NEGOTIATION OBJECTIVES]{.underline}**

**[A. Transaction Overview]{.underline}**

Meridian Advanced Materials, LLC and Kastor Chemical Industries AG are in advanced negotiations to form MKPoly Coatings, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company that will develop, manufacture, and commercialize advanced specialty polymer coatings for industrial and aerospace applications. The JV represents the most significant strategic partnership in Meridian\'s history and, if structured correctly, will substantially enhance Meridian\'s competitive position in the North American specialty coatings market while providing Kastor access to Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations for use in its European and Asian product lines.

The key economic parameters of the proposed transaction, as currently reflected in JVA v.4, are as follows:

**Meridian\'s Contribution --- \$42.9M (55% Equity Interest):**

> • *Intellectual Property Contribution:* \$28.6M, comprising 23 proprietary coating formulation patents, trade secrets, and related know-how, as independently appraised by Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP in its report dated June 9, 2025. These IP assets constitute the technological foundation of MKPoly\'s planned product portfolio and include Meridian\'s flagship CR-7 and CR-9 coating systems, which have no commercially available equivalent in the North American market.
>
> • *Cash Contribution:* \$14.3M in immediately available funds, to be deposited into MKPoly\'s operating account upon formation filing.

**Kastor\'s Contribution --- \$43.8M (45% Equity Interest):**

> • *Cash Contribution:* \$41.8M (€38.0M converted at the agreed reference rate of \$1.10/€), to be funded in two tranches --- 60% at closing and 40% within 90 days of the operational launch date.
>
> • *Supply Agreement Value:* \$2.0M, representing the estimated below-market value of Kastor\'s commitment to supply certain specialty polymer precursors to MKPoly at preferential pricing under a five-year supply agreement (Schedule 4.01(b) to the JVA).

**Total JV Capitalization:** \$86.7M.

**Entity and Governance Structure:** MKPoly will be organized as a Delaware limited liability company, with its registered office at 1209 Kirkwood Highway, Wilmington, DE 19801 (c/o Delaware Registered Agents, Inc.). Operational headquarters will be located at 4710 Industrial Commons Drive, Houston, TX 77045, adjacent to Meridian\'s existing research and manufacturing campus. Day-to-day management will be vested in officers appointed by the Management Committee (the \"Board\"), with governance provisions as discussed in Section III of this Playbook.

**Timeline:** The parties have targeted September 2, 2025 for the formation filing with the Delaware Division of Corporations. Operational launch is planned for Q1 2026, contingent on completion of facility build-out, regulatory approvals, and key personnel recruitment.

**Status of Negotiations:** The parties have exchanged four successive drafts of the JVA since April 2025. Substantial agreement has been reached on approximately 70% of the JVA\'s provisions, including the basic contribution structure, entity formation, member representations and warranties, confidentiality obligations, and initial officer appointments (other than as to the scope of the CEO\'s and CFO\'s authority, which is linked to the governance dispute). Five provisions remain materially contested and are the subject of this Playbook.

**[B. Strategic Priorities for the August 11 Meeting]{.underline}**

The August 11 principals meeting between Cordero and Dr. Laufer represents the most critical negotiation session in the MKPoly formation process. Prior sessions at the working-team level (led by Meridian\'s VP of Corporate Development, James Ortiz, and Kastor\'s Director of Strategic Partnerships, Tomás Escalante) have narrowed the issues but have been unable to resolve the five contested provisions identified below. The principals meeting is intended to achieve resolution or, at minimum, establish parameters within which the working teams can finalize drafting.

Meridian\'s two overriding negotiation objectives for the August 11 meeting are:

1\. **Maintaining Control over IP License-Back Provisions (Section 12.05 of the JVA):** Meridian must prevent Kastor from obtaining sublicensing rights over Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations --- whether the contributed IP itself or co-developed improvements to that IP --- that would allow Kastor to place those formulations in the hands of Meridian\'s North American competitors. This is not merely a commercial preference; it is an existential issue for Meridian\'s standalone business. If Kastor can sublicense Meridian\'s CR-7 and CR-9 coating systems to competitors such as Polaris Coatings Corp., Endurance Surface Technologies, or Trident Polymer Systems, Meridian\'s competitive advantage in the North American specialty coatings market --- the very advantage that makes Meridian an attractive JV partner --- would be fundamentally compromised. Cordero has described this as the \"one thing we cannot give away.\"

2\. **Preserving Meridian\'s Management Control Consistent with Its 55% Equity Position (Section 5.01 of the JVA):** Meridian holds 55% of MKPoly\'s equity and has contributed the JV\'s core technology. It is axiomatic in joint venture practice that the majority equity holder retains operational control, subject to appropriate protective rights for the minority partner. Kastor\'s push for equal board representation and broad supermajority requirements is inconsistent with the economic structure of the deal and, if accepted, would give Kastor an effective veto over operational decisions despite holding only 45% of the equity. Meridian must retain either a board majority or an equivalent structural mechanism (such as a casting vote) that ensures its ability to direct MKPoly\'s day-to-day operations.

These two priorities are **non-fungible** --- they cannot be traded for concessions on other provisions. Rather, concessions on other provisions (non-compete duration, exit valuation methodology, capital call mechanics) should be strategically deployed to protect these two positions. The Concession-Priority Matrix in Section II below provides a structured framework for this approach.

**[C. Cordero\'s Negotiation Mandate]{.underline}**

In preparation for this Playbook, I met with Elaine Cordero on July 25, 2025 to discuss her negotiation parameters for the August 11 meeting. Cordero provided the following internal guidance, which defines the boundaries of Meridian\'s flexibility on each contested provision:

> • **Management Control (Section 5.01):** Cordero is willing to accept Kastor veto rights on Reserved Matters, provided that the list of Reserved Matters is kept narrow and does not extend to ordinary-course business decisions, annual budget approval, or hiring and termination of personnel below the C-suite level. Cordero expressed a preference for maintaining 3-2 board composition but acknowledged that a 3-3 board with a Meridian-appointed chairperson holding a casting vote may be acceptable as a final fallback, provided the casting vote applies to all non-Reserved Matters.
>
> • **Exit Valuation (Sections 10.03--10.04):** Cordero is willing to move from Meridian\'s opening position of a 6.5× trailing 12-month EBITDA multiple to as high as 7.0×. She is not authorized to exceed 7.0× without returning to the Meridian board. She noted that 7.0× is near the median of the Frazier Whitmore comparable-transaction range (6.2×--8.1×, median 7.1×) and is therefore defensible to the Meridian board.
>
> • **Capital Calls (Section 4.03):** Cordero is willing to accept a total cure period of approximately 75 days (combining notice and cure), up from Meridian\'s opening position of 30 days and significantly below Kastor\'s request for 90 days. She is open to a structured penalty-interest mechanism during the cure period.
>
> • **Non-Compete (Section 11.01):** Cordero is prepared to move from Meridian\'s opening position of a 2-year post-exit non-compete to 18 months. She views non-compete duration as available concession currency and has authorized its use to secure gains on IP license-back and management control.
>
> • **IP License-Back (Section 12.05):** Cordero will not accept any structure that allows Kastor to sublicense Meridian-contributed IP to Meridian\'s North American competitors. This is an absolute red line with no authorized flexibility. Cordero described this as follows: \"If they can hand our formulations to Polaris or Trident, there is no deal.\" Limited flexibility exists on co-developed IP licensing outside North America and on the treatment of improvements to contributed IP, as detailed in Section VII below.

**[II. CONCESSION-PRIORITY MATRIX]{.underline}**

**[A. Priority Ranking]{.underline}**

The following priority ranking reflects both Cordero\'s negotiation mandate and our independent assessment of each provision\'s strategic and commercial significance to Meridian. The ranking is organized into three tiers, with Tier 1 representing the highest priority (minimal concession room) and Tier 3 representing the greatest flexibility (available as concession currency to secure gains on higher-priority items).

**Tier 1 --- Protect at All Costs (Highest Priority --- Minimal Concession Room)**

**IP License-Back (Section 12.05):** Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items, independently valued at \$28.6M by Frazier Whitmore & Co., constitute the technological core of MKPoly\'s product portfolio. These assets include Meridian\'s proprietary CR-7 and CR-9 coating formulation systems, related process parameters, and associated trade secrets. Post-exit sublicensing restrictions are Cordero\'s stated red line and Meridian\'s single highest-priority contested term. No sublicensing of Meridian-contributed IP to Meridian\'s North American competitors under any circumstances. Limited concession room exists on co-developed IP licensing and improvements allocation (see Section VII), but these concessions must be carefully structured to prevent indirect access to Meridian\'s contributed formulations.

**Management Control (Section 5.01):** Meridian\'s 55% equity interest must be reflected in the JV\'s governance structure. Board composition and the CEO\'s casting vote in deadlock situations are core mechanisms for maintaining operational direction of MKPoly. Meridian\'s contributed IP is embedded in every JV product line, and technical management by Meridian-aligned leadership is essential to preserving IP integrity, product quality, and customer relationships. Concession room is limited to accepting Kastor protective veto rights on narrow Reserved Matters or, at the outer boundary, a 3-3 board with structural protections (see Section III).

**Tier 2 --- Defend but Trade Selectively (Moderate Priority)**

**Capital Calls & Dilution (Section 4.03):** Meridian\'s strong preference is for uncapped immediate pro-rata dilution upon failure to fund capital calls, which incentivizes prompt funding and rewards Meridian\'s superior financial capacity. However, Meridian has meaningful room to negotiate cure periods, penalty-interest mechanisms, and limited loan alternatives, provided that the economic terms preserve the incentive to fund. This provision is a moderate priority: important to Meridian\'s long-term economic position in the JV, but not existential. Cordero has authorized movement to approximately 75 days total cure, and the provision can serve as a middle-ground item where both parties can claim concessions.

**Tier 3 --- Greater Flexibility (Available as Concession Currency)**

**Non-Compete Duration (Section 11.01):** Meridian\'s opening position is a 2-year post-exit non-compete covering all specialty polymer coatings in North America. Cordero is prepared to move to 18 months --- a 25% reduction in duration that represents a significant, visible concession. Non-compete duration is the primary concession lever available to Meridian to secure gains on Tier 1 items, particularly IP license-back. The concession is low-cost to Meridian because 18 months falls within the safe harbor for enforceability under both Delaware and Texas law and provides adequate competitive protection.

**Exit Valuation Multiple (Sections 10.03--10.04):** Meridian\'s opening position is a 6.5× trailing 12-month EBITDA multiple, which is at the conservative end of the Frazier Whitmore comparable-transaction range (6.2×--8.1×, median 7.1×). Cordero is prepared to move to 7.0×. Movement from 6.5× to 7.0× is a 7.7% increase in the exit valuation multiple, which can be presented to Kastor as a meaningful concession. The actual cost to Meridian depends on MKPoly\'s EBITDA at exit and whether Meridian is the buyer or seller --- if Meridian is the buyer, a higher multiple is costly, but if Meridian is the seller, the higher multiple benefits Meridian. On balance, the exit valuation multiple is a relatively low-cost concession given the uncertainty of its ultimate direction.

**[C. Red Lines Summary]{.underline}**

The following positions are absolute walk-away triggers. If Kastor\'s final position crosses any of these lines, Meridian should terminate negotiations and pursue its BATNA (see Section X).

1\. **No sublicensing by Kastor of Meridian-contributed IP to Schedule 12.05 Restricted Competitors.** This prohibition applies regardless of territory, regardless of whether the sublicense is direct or indirect, and regardless of whether the Restricted Competitor is the end user or an intermediary. Any structure --- including purportedly \"territorial\" sublicensing --- that creates a pathway for Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations to reach Polaris Coatings Corp., Endurance Surface Technologies, Trident Polymer Systems, or any other entity on the Restricted Competitors schedule is unacceptable.

2\. **Meridian must retain effective management control.** This means either (a) a board majority (3-2 composition), or (b) if 3-3 composition, a Meridian-appointed chairperson with casting vote on all non-Reserved Matters and sole Meridian authority to appoint the CEO and CFO. A governance structure in which Kastor holds an effective veto over ordinary-course business decisions is inconsistent with Meridian\'s 55% equity stake and is a deal-breaker.

3\. **Dilution cap cannot exceed the range Meridian can accept.** A dilution cap below 20% per annum effectively subsidizes a non-funding partner and eliminates Meridian\'s economic incentive to pursue additional capital investment. Combined with a governance structure that gives Kastor veto power, a low dilution cap would allow Kastor to block growth initiatives and resist funding without consequence.

4\. **No cumulative concession structure that leaves Meridian with majority equity but minority effective control.** Even if no single red line is crossed, a combination of concessions across all five provisions that, taken together, gives Kastor effective veto power, caps Meridian\'s dilution upside, broadly licenses Meridian\'s IP, and constrains Meridian\'s post-exit competitive flexibility would be unacceptable. Cordero and the deal team should evaluate the aggregate effect of any package proposal before accepting.

**[III. CONTESTED PROVISION #1 --- MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE & GOVERNANCE (Section 5.01)]{.underline}**

**[A. Meridian\'s Opening / Preferred Position]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s preferred governance structure, as set forth in its initial markup of Section 5.01, is as follows:

> • **Board Composition:** The Management Committee (the \"Board\") shall consist of five (5) members --- three (3) appointed by Meridian (the \"Meridian Designees\") and two (2) appointed by Kastor (the \"Kastor Designees\"). Each member shall appoint and remove its designees at its sole discretion.
>
> • **Voting:** All Board actions shall require approval by simple majority vote of the members present at a duly convened meeting at which a quorum is present. A quorum shall consist of four (4) members, including at least one Meridian Designee and one Kastor Designee.
>
> • **CEO Casting Vote:** In the event of a deadlock (defined as a matter on which the Board is unable to reach a majority vote after two successive meetings at which the matter is considered), the Chief Executive Officer, who shall be appointed by Meridian, shall hold a casting vote to resolve the deadlock.
>
> • **Officer Appointments:** The CEO and Chief Financial Officer shall be appointed by Meridian. The Chief Operating Officer and Chief Technology Officer shall be appointed jointly by the Board (simple majority vote). All other officers below the C-suite level shall be appointed by the CEO in the ordinary course of business.

**Rationale:** Meridian\'s 55% equity interest and the fact that its contributed IP (\$28.6M, representing the JV\'s core technological platform) forms the foundation of MKPoly\'s product lines justify proportionate governance control. In the specialty chemicals industry, joint ventures in which one partner holds a clear majority equity position --- particularly when that partner has contributed the venture\'s core technology --- consistently grant governance control to the majority holder. The 3-2 board composition is the standard mechanism for achieving this, and Meridian\'s proposal is fully consistent with industry norms and Delaware LLC law, which permits broad flexibility in allocating governance rights through the LLC agreement.

Moreover, the CEO casting vote serves a practical function: it provides a clear, efficient deadlock resolution mechanism that avoids the delay and expense of mediation, arbitration, or forced buyout proceedings. The alternative --- protracted deadlock resolution processes --- creates operational uncertainty that is harmful to MKPoly and its employees, customers, and suppliers.

**[B. Fallback Ladder]{.underline}**

**First Concession --- Protective Veto Rights (Narrow Reserved Matters)**

If Kastor insists on enhanced protective rights --- either by demanding a sixth board seat, by demanding supermajority requirements on all Board actions, or by conditioning acceptance of the 3-2 structure on broader minority protections --- Meridian\'s first fallback is to maintain the 3-2 board composition but concede supermajority voting (requiring the affirmative vote of at least four members, which necessarily includes at least one Kastor Designee) on a narrowly defined list of Reserved Matters.

The Reserved Matters list should be limited to the following:

> \(a\) Any amendment, modification, or restatement of the LLC Agreement or the Certificate of Formation;
>
> \(b\) Voluntary dissolution, winding up, or filing of a petition under any federal or state bankruptcy, insolvency, or receivership statute;
>
> \(c\) Sale, transfer, or other disposition of all or substantially all of MKPoly\'s assets in a single transaction or a series of related transactions;
>
> \(d\) Any single-transaction capital expenditure (or series of related capital expenditures within any 12-month period) exceeding \$5,000,000;
>
> \(e\) Admission of any new member to MKPoly, or any issuance of new membership interests to any person or entity other than the existing members on a pro-rata basis;
>
> \(f\) Entry into, or material amendment of, any transaction between MKPoly and any member or affiliate of a member (related-party transactions); and
>
> \(g\) Any material change to the nature or scope of MKPoly\'s business as described in Section 2.04 of the LLC Agreement.

**Cordero\'s internal guidance supports this concession** provided the Reserved Matters list does not expand to include any of the following: ordinary-course operating decisions, approval of annual operating budgets, hiring or termination of personnel below the C-suite level, entry into customer or supplier contracts in the ordinary course of business, pricing decisions, or product development priorities. These are operational matters that must remain within the CEO\'s delegated authority or subject to simple-majority Board vote.

**Presentation strategy:** This concession should be presented as a meaningful move by Meridian. Cordero should emphasize that the Reserved Matters list gives Kastor an effective veto on every existential decision --- dissolution, asset sales, new partners, major capital commitments, and related-party transactions. These are precisely the decisions where a minority partner\'s protective interests are legitimate.
