If Kastor does identify such a sublicensee, Meridian should evaluate whether that entity can be removed from Schedule 12.05 without compromising Meridian\'s competitive position --- but only if the entity genuinely does not compete with Meridian in North America.

**Kastor Argument 3 --- Symmetry**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor\'s counsel, Tomás Escalante, will argue that any sublicensing restriction on Kastor should apply equally to Meridian. If Kastor cannot sublicense to Meridian\'s competitors, then Meridian should not be able to sublicense to Kastor\'s competitors. The principle of \"equal treatment for equal partners\" is a frequent Kastor theme.

**Recommended response:** Accept symmetry on co-developed IP sublicensing. The Second Concession already structures this symmetrically: Kastor can sublicense in Europe and Asia, Meridian can sublicense in the Americas, and neither party can sublicense to the other\'s listed competitors. This reciprocal structure is easy to administer and gives Kastor the \"equal treatment\" it seeks.

However, reject symmetry on contributed IP. The parties\' contributed IP items are different in nature (Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations vs. Kastor\'s manufacturing process documentation), different in independently appraised value (\$28.6M vs. not appraised), and different in competitive sensitivity (Meridian\'s formulations are the JV\'s core differentiator; Kastor\'s process documentation is operationally important but not competitively unique). Symmetric treatment of fundamentally asymmetric contributions is not \"equal\" --- it is inequitable.

Cordero should frame this clearly: \"Symmetry applies when the underlying assets are comparable. Our coating formulations are not comparable to your process documentation --- they are worth 14 times more, they are the reason this JV exists, and they require different protections. We have offered you a very broad license to co-developed IP on symmetric terms. What we cannot do is treat our contributed IP the same as yours when the contributions are not the same.\"

**[VIII. COMPARABLE TRANSACTION BENCHMARKS]{.underline}**

The following comparable-transaction summaries are drawn from the Frazier Whitmore & Co. specialty chemicals transaction database and from publicly available information supplemented by our firm\'s deal experience. All three transactions involve formation of specialty chemicals JVs with structures comparable to MKPoly. Confidential details have been omitted where required by third-party confidentiality obligations.

**[A. Comparable Transaction #1 --- Specialty Coatings JV (2023)]{.underline}**

**Transaction Overview:** Formation of a specialty coatings JV between a U.S.-based coatings manufacturer and a European chemicals company. The JV was focused on advanced protective coatings for energy-sector applications. The U.S. partner contributed proprietary coating technology and customer relationships; the European partner contributed capital and access to international markets.

**Key Terms:**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Provision**                       **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Equity Split                        60% (U.S. partner) / 40% (European partner)

  Exit EBITDA Multiple                6.8× trailing 12-month EBITDA

  Non-Compete                         18 months post-exit, covering JV product lines and closely adjacent products, geographic scope limited to North America and Western Europe

  Board Composition                   3-2 (majority partner appoints 3), with minority protective rights on 5 specified Reserved Matters (amendment, dissolution, asset sale, capex \>\$3M, new members)

  Capital Call Cure                   45-day cure period, uncapped dilution upon expiration

  IP License-Back                     Contributed IP exclusive license-back to contributing party upon dissolution; no sublicensing by either party of contributed IP; co-developed IP licensed non-exclusively to both parties with no sublicensing
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Relevance to MKPoly:** This transaction is the closest comparable to MKPoly in terms of equity structure (60/40 vs. 55/45), technology contribution profile (one partner contributing proprietary coatings IP), and industry segment (specialty coatings). The terms on every key dimension --- 3-2 board, 6.8× multiple, 18-month non-compete, 45-day cure, no contributed IP sublicensing --- support Meridian\'s positions or fall within Meridian\'s concession range.

**[B. Comparable Transaction #2 --- Advanced Materials JV (2024)]{.underline}**

**Transaction Overview:** Formation of an advanced materials JV between a U.S. specialty chemicals company and a Japanese materials conglomerate. The JV was focused on high-performance polymer composites for automotive and electronics applications. The U.S. partner contributed formulation technology and North American manufacturing capacity; the Japanese partner contributed capital, Asian market access, and manufacturing process expertise.

**Key Terms:**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Provision**                       **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Equity Split                        55% (U.S. partner) / 45% (Japanese partner)

  Exit EBITDA Multiple                7.4× trailing 12-month EBITDA

  Non-Compete                         24 months post-exit, broad scope (all polymer composites), North America and Asia

  Board Composition                   3-3 (equal representation), majority-appointed chairperson with casting vote on non-Reserved Matters; majority partner retains sole right to appoint CEO and CFO

  Capital Call Cure                   60-day cure period; dilution capped at 15% per annum with automatic forced-sale mechanism if dilution exceeds 15% in any 24-month period

  IP License-Back                     Contributed IP reverts to contributing party; co-developed IP licensed non-exclusively to both parties with territorial sublicensing restrictions (U.S. partner: Americas; Japanese partner: Asia-Pacific); mutual sublicensing restrictions preventing licensing to named competitors
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Relevance to MKPoly:** This transaction has the identical equity structure to MKPoly (55/45) and involves similar contribution dynamics (technology + capital). Notably, even with a 3-3 board, the majority partner retained a casting vote and sole officer-appointment authority --- precisely Meridian\'s Second Concession position on governance. The 7.4× multiple is above Meridian\'s authorized ceiling but validates the upper end of the comparable range. The territorial sublicensing structure for co-developed IP mirrors Meridian\'s Second Concession on IP license-back.

**[C. Comparable Transaction #3 --- Polymer Manufacturing JV (2024)]{.underline}**

**Transaction Overview:** Formation of a polymer manufacturing JV between two global chemicals companies with equal equity contributions. The JV was focused on commodity and specialty polymer production for packaging and industrial applications. Both partners contributed significant capital; one partner contributed proprietary polymerization technology, and the other contributed feedstock supply agreements and manufacturing infrastructure.

**Key Terms:**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Provision**                       **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Equity Split                        50% / 50%

  Exit EBITDA Multiple                7.1× trailing 12-month EBITDA, determined by baseball arbitration (each party selects one appraiser, those two select a third; third appraiser\'s determination is binding)

  Non-Compete                         12 months post-exit, narrow scope (specific JV product lines only), geography limited to territories with active JV sales

  Board Composition                   Equal board (3-3), with mediation-then-forced-buyout deadlock mechanism; no casting vote

  Capital Call Cure                   60-day cure period, uncapped dilution

  IP License-Back                     Co-developed IP licensed exclusively to JV during term; upon dissolution, non-exclusively to both parties with mutual sublicensing restrictions (neither party may sublicense to named competitors of the other)
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Relevance to MKPoly:** This transaction is less directly comparable because of the 50/50 equity split (MKPoly is 55/45), which explains the equal board without a casting vote. However, the 7.1× EBITDA multiple (determined by baseball arbitration) validates the market center and supports Meridian\'s movement to 7.0×. The mutual sublicensing restrictions on co-developed IP confirm that sublicensing protections are standard in specialty chemicals JVs, even between equal partners.

**[D. Takeaways for Negotiation]{.underline}**

The three comparable transactions, taken together, provide strong empirical support for Meridian\'s positions on each contested provision:

**Non-Compete Duration:** An 18-month non-compete is squarely within market range. Comparable #1 used 18 months, Comparable #2 used 24 months, and Comparable #3 used 12 months. The median is 18 months, which is precisely Meridian\'s first concession position. Cordero should cite this data when presenting the 18-month offer.

**Exit Valuation Multiple:** The comparable range is 6.8×--7.4×, with a median of 7.1×. Meridian\'s 6.5× opening is below the range; Meridian\'s 7.0× authorized ceiling is near the median. Movement to 7.0× is clearly market-appropriate and can be presented as conceding to market center.

**Board Composition:** In both 60/40 and 55/45 transactions (Comparables #1 and #2), the majority partner retained governance control --- either through a 3-2 board (Comparable #1) or through a 3-3 board with casting vote and sole officer-appointment authority (Comparable #2). Only the 50/50 transaction (Comparable #3) used an equal board without a casting vote. This confirms that Meridian\'s 55% equity position justifies either a 3-2 board or, at minimum, a 3-3 board with structural control mechanisms.

**Sublicensing Restrictions:** Contributed IP sublicensing restrictions are universal in comparable transactions. No comparable transaction permitted sublicensing of contributed IP. Co-developed IP sublicensing restrictions (territorial limitations, named-competitor prohibitions) appeared in two of three transactions (Comparables #2 and #3). Meridian\'s positions on IP license-back are firmly within market norms.

**Capital Call Cure Period:** Cure periods of 45--60 days are standard. No comparable transaction used a cure period shorter than 45 days or longer than 60 days. Meridian\'s second concession (60 days with penalty interest) is at the top of the market range but is supported by the data.

**[IX. CROSS-PROVISION NEGOTIATION STRATEGY & SEQUENCING]{.underline}**

**[A. Recommended Sequencing for August 11 Meeting]{.underline}**

The order in which provisions are discussed at the principals meeting will significantly influence outcomes. Negotiations that begin with high-stakes, deeply contested items risk early impasse and adversarial dynamics that poison subsequent discussions. Negotiations that begin with areas of potential agreement build momentum, establish trust, and create a pattern of reciprocal concession that makes later agreement on harder issues more likely.

**Recommended order of discussion:**

**1. Non-Compete (Section 11.01) --- Start Here.** Open with the non-compete because it is Meridian\'s Tier 3 item with the greatest concession room and the most visible movement. Cordero should lead by offering to reduce the non-compete from 2 years to 18 months --- a concession Kastor has identified as important and that Cordero has pre-approved. This sets a cooperative tone and establishes Meridian as a willing, flexible negotiation partner. It also creates a clear credit: Kastor now owes a reciprocal concession.

**2. Exit Valuation (Sections 10.03--10.04) --- Build Momentum.** Proceed to exit valuation, another Tier 3 item. Offer movement from 6.5× to 6.8× (or, if the non-compete concession generated significant goodwill, to 7.0× immediately). Present the movement as data-driven. If Kastor raises the appraiser-independence issue, offer the hybrid mechanism or baseball arbitration with collar. By the end of this discussion, Meridian should have offered two visible concessions, and Kastor should feel that the negotiation is productive and that Meridian is acting in good faith.

**3. IP License-Back (Section 12.05) --- Highest Priority, Negotiate from Strength.** With two Tier 3 concessions on the table, Cordero is now in the strongest possible position to hold firm on IP license-back. The message is: \"We\'ve moved significantly on non-compete and valuation. On IP licensing, we have a red line that I need to explain.\" Present Meridian\'s opening position on contributed IP (no license to Kastor, no sublicensing under any circumstances) and offer the First Concession on co-developed IP (non-exclusive license to Kastor outside North America with Restricted Competitors restrictions). Frame the co-developed IP concession as responsive to Kastor\'s stated top priority while making clear that contributed IP is non-negotiable.

**4. Management Control (Section 5.01) --- Frame as Partnership, Not Control.** After the IP license-back discussion, turn to governance. By this point, Cordero has demonstrated flexibility on two items and firmness on one, which establishes credibility. Present the 3-2 board with narrow Reserved Matters as the preferred position. If Kastor pushes back, offer the 3-3 board with casting vote as the final fallback. Link governance to IP: \"Our board composition reflects our need to protect the technology we\'ve brought to this partnership. If we have operational control, we\'re comfortable with the co-developed IP licensing framework we just discussed.\"

**5. Capital Calls (Section 4.03) --- Close on Middle Ground.** Address capital calls last. This is a Tier 2 item where both sides have room to move, and it provides a natural closing discussion where both parties can claim concessions. Offer the 60-day cure period with penalty interest. If Kastor insists on a loan alternative, offer the punitive-terms loan. End the meeting on a constructive note with an item that can be resolved through give-and-take.

**[B. Package Deal Proposals]{.underline}**

If the item-by-item discussion does not produce resolution, Cordero may need to present a package deal --- a comprehensive proposal that addresses all five provisions simultaneously. Package proposals force trade-offs and prevent the parties from optimizing each provision independently (which tends to produce deadlock, as each party maximizes its position on each item without considering the overall picture).

**[C. Linkage Strategy]{.underline}**

The following instructions are for Cordero\'s use during the negotiation:

**1. Link IP concessions to governance gains.** If Kastor asks for concessions on IP license-back (e.g., broader sublicensing rights, weaker Restricted Competitors protections), require reciprocal concessions on management control. The specific language Cordero can use: \"We\'re open to discussing broader co-developed IP licensing, but that discussion only works for us if governance reflects our majority position. If you want more flexibility on IP, we need the 3-2 board.\" This linkage is logical: broader IP licensing increases Meridian\'s risk, and governance control is the mechanism by which Meridian manages that risk.

**2. Never concede on both Tier 1 items simultaneously.** If Cordero moves on governance (from 3-2 to 3-3 with casting vote), she must not simultaneously concede on IP licensing (e.g., by weakening the Restricted Competitors covenant or the revenue-based negative covenant). Each Tier 1 concession must be funded by a Kastor concession on the other Tier 1 item or on a Tier 2/3 item of equivalent value.

**3. Use Tier 3 concessions proactively.** Do not wait for Kastor to ask for non-compete or valuation concessions. Offer them proactively and early, establishing the pattern of reciprocity before the conversation turns to Tier 1 items. A concession offered freely is worth more in negotiation goodwill than the same concession extracted under pressure.

**4. Avoid incremental negotiation on IP.** The IP license-back provisions should be presented as a package (opening position plus available concessions), not negotiated incrementally. Incremental negotiation creates the impression that every element of Meridian\'s IP position is movable, inviting Kastor to push on each element individually. Present the co-developed IP licensing framework as a carefully structured package where the territorial restrictions, Restricted Competitors schedule, and revenue-based negative covenant work together as a system. Removing any one element undermines the whole.

**5. If Kastor presents a package, evaluate holistically.** Do not respond to each element of a Kastor package individually. Take the full package under advisement, request a break if necessary, and evaluate the aggregate effect before responding. This prevents the negotiation from devolving into a point-by-point critique that produces adversarial dynamics.

**[X. WALK-AWAY ANALYSIS]{.underline}**

**[A. Deal-Breaker Scenarios]{.underline}**

There are three scenarios under which Meridian should terminate negotiations and decline to proceed with the MKPoly formation:

**Scenario 1 --- Kastor Sublicensing of Contributed IP.** If Kastor obtains any sublicensing right --- direct or indirect, limited or unlimited, territorial or global --- over any of Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items (\$28.6M in appraised value), Meridian should walk away. The contributed IP is Meridian\'s core competitive asset. Sublicensing to any third party, whether a named competitor or not, creates an uncontrollable dissemination risk. Once a sublicense is granted, Meridian cannot control how the sublicensee uses the technology, whether the sublicensee further disseminates it, or whether the technology eventually reaches Meridian\'s competitors through secondary transfers, personnel movement, or reverse engineering. This risk is existential and justifies deal termination.

**Scenario 2 --- Loss of Effective Management Control.** If the final governance structure gives Kastor an effective veto over ordinary-course business decisions (e.g., through broad supermajority requirements, equal board without casting vote, or joint CEO appointment) and Meridian has no casting vote or equivalent tiebreak mechanism, Meridian should walk away. A governance structure in which a 45% partner can block operating decisions --- pricing, product development, key hires, customer strategy --- is not a joint venture; it is a hostage situation. Meridian\'s 55% equity position was negotiated to provide operational control, and a governance structure that nullifies this control is inconsistent with the economic terms of the deal.

**Scenario 3 --- Cumulative Concession Overload.** Even if no single red line is crossed, a combination of concessions across all five provisions that, taken together, leaves Meridian with majority equity but minority effective control is a deal-breaker. For example: a 3-3 board with broad supermajority requirements (losing management control), a 10% dilution cap with market-rate loan alternative (losing capital call leverage), a 12-month narrow non-compete (losing competitive protection), and broad co-developed IP sublicensing with weak Restricted Competitors protections (weakening IP protection) --- even if contributed IP sublicensing is prohibited --- would collectively leave Meridian in a position worse than its BATNA. Cordero should evaluate any package proposal against this cumulative standard.

**[B. BATNA Assessment]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s best alternative to a negotiated agreement with Kastor is to proceed independently with its proprietary coating formulations, without the JV. This alternative is suboptimal but viable:

**What Meridian Retains Without the JV:**

> • Full ownership and control of all 23 contributed IP items (\$28.6M value), including the CR-7 and CR-9 coating systems
>
> • All existing customer relationships in North American specialty coatings
>
> • All existing manufacturing infrastructure at the Houston campus
>
> • \$14.3M in cash that would have been contributed to MKPoly, available for redeployment

**What Meridian Loses Without the JV:**

> • Access to Kastor\'s manufacturing process documentation (9 contributed IP items), which Meridian would need to develop or acquire independently. Estimated timeline: 18--24 months. Estimated cost: \$15M--\$20M in capital expenditure, comprising facility modifications, process engineering, and personnel recruitment.
>
> • Access to Kastor\'s European distribution network, which would have provided MKPoly with an established channel for international sales. Meridian would need to build its own European distribution capability or partner with a different distribution partner. Estimated timeline: 12--18 months. Estimated cost: \$5M--\$8M.
>
> • Kastor\'s cash contribution (\$41.8M), which would have funded the majority of MKPoly\'s initial operations. Without this funding, Meridian would need to finance independently or seek alternative financing. Meridian\'s current revolving credit facility provides up to \$50M in available capacity, which is sufficient but would increase Meridian\'s leverage ratio.

**Net Assessment:** The JV remains preferable to independent development --- it accelerates Meridian\'s entry into advanced polymer manufacturing by approximately 18--24 months and provides access to Kastor\'s capital and distribution capabilities. However, the JV is not essential to Meridian\'s core business. Meridian can succeed independently, and the walk-away option is credible. This credibility strengthens Meridian\'s negotiating position.

**[C. Kastor\'s BATNA Assessment]{.underline}**

Understanding Kastor\'s alternatives is essential to assessing Meridian\'s leverage. Our analysis, based on publicly available information and intelligence gathered during due diligence, suggests that Kastor\'s BATNA is significantly weaker than Meridian\'s:

**What Kastor Lacks Without the JV:**

> • Proprietary coating formulations. Kastor does not have coating technology comparable to Meridian\'s CR-7 and CR-9 systems. Kastor\'s existing coatings product line consists of commodity-grade protective coatings --- functional but not differentiated. To compete in the advanced specialty coatings market, Kastor would need to develop proprietary formulations through independent R&D. Estimated timeline: 3--5 years, with no guarantee of equivalent performance. Estimated cost: \$30M--\$50M.
>
> • North American market access. Kastor\'s North American presence in specialty polymer coatings is minimal. Its existing sales are primarily in Europe and Asia. Building a North American customer base from scratch --- particularly in the relationship-driven aerospace and industrial segments --- would require significant investment in sales infrastructure, technical support, and customer qualification. Estimated timeline: 2--3 years. Estimated cost: \$10M--\$15M.

**What Kastor Retains Without the JV:**

> • Its €38M in cash, available for redeployment to other projects (including the Ludwigshafen expansion and the South Korea JV)
>
> • Its manufacturing process documentation and polymer precursor supply chain
>
> • Its European and Asian distribution networks

**Asymmetry Assessment:** Kastor needs Meridian\'s IP more than Meridian needs Kastor\'s cash. Meridian\'s coating formulations are the JV\'s raison d\'être --- without them, there is no MKPoly. Kastor\'s cash contribution, while substantial, is fungible: Meridian could, in principle, raise equivalent capital through debt financing or alternative partnerships. Kastor cannot replicate Meridian\'s IP through any means other than multi-year R&D at uncertain cost and outcome.

This asymmetry is Meridian\'s most important source of negotiating leverage on IP license-back. Cordero should be prepared to invoke it, carefully, if Kastor pushes hard on sublicensing: \"We understand your interest in broad licensing, but let\'s be candid about what each of us brings to this partnership. Our formulations are the reason we\'re sitting at this table. We\'re not going to put them at risk.\"

**[APPENDIX B --- SCHEDULE 12.05 RESTRICTED COMPETITORS (PROPOSED DRAFT)]{.underline}**

**[Framework and Methodology]{.underline}**

Schedule 12.05 of the JVA will identify the Restricted Competitors to which neither party may sublicense co-developed IP (or, in the Second Concession structure, to which the revenue-based negative covenant applies as a supplementary restriction). The schedule serves as the primary enforcement mechanism for Meridian\'s IP sublicensing protections and must be drafted with care.

The following framework should be used to populate the schedule:

**Inclusion Criteria:** An entity should be listed on Schedule 12.05 if it meets any of the following criteria:

> \(a\) The entity derives 10% or more of its consolidated annual revenue from the manufacture, sale, or distribution of specialty polymer coatings in North America;
>
> \(b\) The entity has been identified by Meridian\'s competitive intelligence function as a direct competitor in any of MKPoly\'s target product categories (advanced protective coatings, performance coatings for aerospace applications, industrial specialty coatings);
>
> \(c\) The entity has publicly announced or is reasonably believed to be developing coating formulations competitive with Meridian\'s CR-7 or CR-9 systems; or
>
> \(d\) The entity is a controlled affiliate (defined as an entity in which the listed competitor owns, directly or indirectly, 50% or more of the outstanding voting equity) of any entity meeting criteria (a), (b), or (c).

Meridian\'s recommended mechanism: either party may propose additions to the schedule by written notice; additions become effective 60 days after notice unless the other party objects, in which case the dispute is resolved by the Board (subject to the casting-vote provision on non-Reserved Matters).

The 50% threshold is recommended as the standard for control, but Meridian should consider a lower threshold (e.g., 25%) if there is concern that a Restricted Competitor could structure a sublicensing arrangement through a minority-owned affiliate.
>

**Revenue-Based Negative Covenant (Supplementary Protection):**

In addition to the named Restricted Competitors on Schedule 12.05, the Second Concession IP license-back structure includes a revenue-based negative covenant: Kastor covenants not to sublicense co-developed IP to any entity that derives more than 25% of its consolidated annual revenue from the manufacture, sale, or distribution of specialty polymer coatings in North America. This covenant operates as a dynamic anti-circumvention mechanism that captures entities not named on Schedule 12.05, including:

> • New market entrants formed after the schedule was finalized
>
> • Existing companies that expand into specialty polymer coatings after the schedule was finalized
>
> • Companies that restructure their operations to avoid triggering the named-entity list
>
> • Foreign companies with significant North American coatings operations that were not identified during initial schedule preparation

The 25% revenue threshold is calibrated to capture entities with material North American coatings exposure while avoiding overinclusiveness. At 25%, the covenant captures any company for which North American specialty polymer coatings represent a significant line of business, while excluding diversified chemicals companies for which coatings are a minor, incidental product category. Meridian should resist any Kastor attempt to raise this threshold above 33%, as higher thresholds would exclude mid-sized competitors with substantial but not dominant coatings operations.

**MEMORANDUM**

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

  **TO:**                             **Elaine Cordero, Chief Executive Officer, Meridian Advanced Materials, LLC**

  **FROM:**                           Brianna Falk, Partner, Whitfield Ames & Trotter LLP

  **DATE:**                           August 4, 2025

  **RE:**                             Enforceability of Post-Exit Non-Competition Covenants in JV/LLC Agreements --- Survey of Delaware, Texas, New York, and California Law

  **MATTER:**                         MKPoly Coatings, LLC --- Joint Venture Agreement (MKPoly JVA v.4)
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

You have asked us to provide background legal analysis supporting Meridian Advanced Materials, LLC\'s (\"Meridian\") negotiation of Section 11.01 (Non-Competition) of the proposed Joint Venture Agreement for MKPoly Coatings, LLC (\"MKPoly\" or the \"JV\") (the \"MKPoly JVA,\" referencing the current draft dated July 22, 2025, version 4). This memorandum is intended to inform Meridian\'s negotiating position in advance of the August 11, 2025 principals meeting with Dr. Henrik Laufer, Chief Strategy Officer of Kastor Chemical Industries AG (\"Kastor\"), and his counsel at Rennert Krieger Solano LLP.

**Summary of Conclusions.** Based on our survey of the law in Delaware, Texas, New York, and California --- the four jurisdictions most likely to be implicated by MKPoly\'s formation, operations, and the parties\' respective commercial activities --- we conclude that an **18-month post-exit non-competition covenant**, confined to **North America (or, more precisely, the JV\'s active-sales territories)** and covering **specialty polymer coatings produced, marketed, or under active development by MKPoly at the time of exit (plus substantially similar or directly competitive products)**, sits comfortably within the safe-harbor parameters recognized by courts in **Delaware** and **Texas** --- the two jurisdictions most likely to govern MKPoly given its Delaware formation and Houston, Texas operational headquarters. New York would also enforce such a covenant under a comparable, and in some respects more permissive, reasonableness analysis. California poses unique and material risks due to its general statutory prohibition on covenants not to compete (Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600), though a recognized LLC/partnership exception (§ 16602.5) may preserve enforceability in the specific JV exit context, provided the covenant is carefully drafted to satisfy the exception\'s requirements.

We note that Meridian\'s opening position --- a two-year duration covering all specialty polymer coatings across all of North America --- and Kastor\'s opening position --- a one-year duration limited to specific JV product lines and confined to territories where MKPoly had active sales at exit --- both fall within the range of enforceable outcomes in the primary governing jurisdictions. This overlap gives Meridian meaningful room to compromise without sacrificing enforceability. Your identified acceptable compromise of 18 months is well-supported by the case law and statutory frameworks surveyed below.

For a quick-reference comparison of enforceable parameters across all four jurisdictions, please refer to the summary safe-harbor table set forth in **Section VII** below.

The remainder of this memorandum sets forth the detailed analysis underlying these conclusions. We address the three principal dimensions of reasonableness --- temporal scope, geographic scope, and product/activity scope --- on a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction basis, followed by an analysis of the reformation and blue-pencil doctrines that determine how courts will treat a covenant found partially overbroad. We then synthesize our recommendations into a proposed compromise formulation and identify additional protective drafting measures for Section 11.01 of the MKPoly JVA.

**[II. Scope and Methodology]{.underline}**

**Surveyed Jurisdictions.** We have surveyed the law of four jurisdictions, each of which bears a direct nexus to the MKPoly transaction and could, under varying circumstances, be called upon to adjudicate the enforceability of the non-competition covenant in Section 11.01:

> • **Delaware.** MKPoly will be formed as a Delaware limited liability company, with its registered office at 1209 Kirkwood Highway, Wilmington, DE 19801, and its registered agent being Capitol Statutory Services, Inc. The JVA\'s governing-law clause, as currently drafted in Section 12.09, designates Delaware law. The Delaware Limited Liability Company Act (6 Del. C. §§ 18-101 *et seq.*) governs the internal affairs of MKPoly, including the rights and obligations of its members upon exit or dissolution. Delaware is therefore the primary jurisdiction for enforceability analysis.
>
> • **Texas.** MKPoly\'s operational headquarters will be located at 4710 Industrial Commons Drive, Houston, TX 77045, which will house the JV\'s coatings development laboratory, manufacturing coordination office, and commercial leadership team. The JV\'s employees, including its initial complement of approximately 35 technical and commercial staff, will be based in Texas. The Texas Covenants Not to Compete Act (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §§ 15.50--15.52) applies to ancillary enforcement questions, and Texas courts may be called upon to enforce or adjudicate the covenant if an enforcement action is brought in the jurisdiction where the JV\'s day-to-day operations occur.
>
> • **New York.** Outside counsel for Kastor in this transaction, Rennert Krieger Solano LLP, is based in New York City. Kastor may, as a negotiating matter, seek to designate New York as an alternative governing-law jurisdiction or, at minimum, as a permissible forum for disputes. New York\'s substantial body of commercial non-compete jurisprudence --- developed in both the Appellate Division and the Court of Appeals --- provides useful comparative authority and represents the standards that would apply if the parties agree to New York governing law or if enforcement is sought against Kastor personnel with New York contacts.
>
> • **California.** Kastor maintains West Coast distribution channels through its subsidiary, Kastor Specialty Products, Inc., based in Los Angeles, California. Additionally, Meridian\'s own supply chain includes California-based raw material suppliers and logistics partners. Any enforcement action against a departing JV member --- or an affiliate of such member --- with California operations or contacts must account for California\'s near-absolute prohibition on non-competition covenants (Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600) and the narrow LLC/partnership exceptions codified in §§ 16601--16602.5. California law is therefore relevant not as a likely choice-of-law jurisdiction, but as a potential obstacle to enforcement in what may be a necessary forum.

**Analytical Framework.** Our analysis addresses the three dimensions of reasonableness that courts in each surveyed jurisdiction apply when evaluating the enforceability of post-exit non-competition covenants:

1\. **Temporal Scope (Duration).** The length of the post-exit restraint period. Courts assess whether the duration is no longer than reasonably necessary to protect the promisee\'s legitimate interest in the goodwill, confidential information, and competitive position acquired through the transaction.

2\. **Geographic Scope.** The territorial extent of the restraint. Courts evaluate whether the geographic limitation corresponds to the areas in which the business actually operates, sells, or has established goodwill, as distinguished from areas where the business has no presence and no protectable interest.

3\. **Product/Activity Scope.** The range of business activities from which the departing member is restrained. Courts examine whether the restrained activities correspond to the business of the joint venture or transferred entity, rather than extending to unrelated product lines or industries.

In addition, we address the **reformation and blue-pencil doctrines** that determine whether and how a court will modify a covenant found to be partially overbroad. These doctrines vary significantly across the four jurisdictions and have important implications for drafting strategy.

**Critical Distinction: Sale-of-Business vs. Employment Non-Competes**

Throughout this memorandum, we emphasize that the analysis focuses on **non-competes ancillary to the sale or dissolution of a business interest** --- specifically, the disposition of an LLC membership interest upon a triggering exit event. This category of restraint receives substantially more favorable treatment in all surveyed jurisdictions than do employment non-competes, which are subject to heightened skepticism and, in many jurisdictions, more restrictive enforceability standards. The rationale for this distinction is well established: a seller of a business interest typically receives substantial consideration for the promise not to compete and is in a position of roughly equal bargaining power with the buyer, whereas an employee may be subject to coercion and may have limited alternatives. The MKPoly non-compete, structured as it is around the exit of a JV member from a commercially negotiated joint venture, falls squarely within the more permissive sale-of-business framework.

**[III. Duration --- Permissible Temporal Scope]{.underline}**

The temporal scope of the non-competition covenant is the single dimension most frequently litigated and the dimension on which the parties\' current positions most clearly diverge (Meridian: 2 years; Kastor: 1 year; identified compromise: 18 months). We therefore address it first and in the greatest detail.

**A. Delaware.** Delaware courts generally enforce post-exit non-competition periods of **one to two years** in commercial and sale-of-business contexts as presumptively reasonable. The Delaware Court of Chancery, which exercises equitable jurisdiction over disputes arising under LLC operating agreements and related covenants, has developed a pragmatic, fact-intensive approach to temporal reasonableness that focuses on the nature of the underlying transaction and the protectable interest at stake.

**Key Case Authority.**

In *EDIX Media Group, Inc. v. Mahani*, the Court of Chancery upheld a two-year post-closing non-competition covenant ancillary to the acquisition of a media technology business. The court observed that the protectable interest in preventing the seller from immediately undermining the goodwill transferred to the buyer justified a \"meaningful but not indefinite\" restraint period. The court noted that a two-year period was sufficient to allow the buyer to establish independent relationships with the acquired company\'s customers and to demonstrate the value of the acquired business under new ownership, while not being so long as to effectively prevent the seller from ever re-entering the market. The court specifically distinguished this sale-of-business context from an employment restraint, observing that the seller had received substantial consideration --- in the form of the purchase price for its equity --- for the non-competition obligation.

In *Boulden v. Stilwell*, the Court of Chancery addressed reasonableness factors in the context of an LLC operating agreement. The court drew a clear distinction between employment restraints, which are \"scrutinized more heavily\" under Delaware law, and restraints incident to the sale or transfer of a membership interest, which are \"given latitude comparable to sale-of-business covenants.\" The court reasoned that where a member of an LLC agrees, as part of the commercial bargain embodied in the operating agreement, to refrain from competition upon exit, that agreement is the product of arm\'s-length negotiation and is supported by the consideration inherent in the membership interest itself.

The Delaware Chancery\'s general analytical framework for assessing the temporal reasonableness of non-competition covenants involves a two-step inquiry: (i) *Is the restraint ancillary to a legitimate transaction?* If the covenant is part of an arm\'s-length sale of a business or membership interest, this element is satisfied. (ii) *Is the duration no greater than reasonably necessary to protect the purchaser\'s (or continuing member\'s) investment in goodwill?* This inquiry is context-dependent and considers the nature of the business, the competitive dynamics of the industry, the relationship between the parties, and the extent of the departing party\'s knowledge of the business\'s confidential information and customer relationships.

**Application to MKPoly.** A post-exit non-compete triggered by a change of control, material breach, deadlock, or involuntary transfer --- the proposed trigger events under negotiation for Section 11.01 --- would be treated under Delaware law as ancillary to the disposition of a membership interest, not as an employment restraint. The covenant would therefore receive the more deferential sale-of-business standard of review. Under this standard, both Meridian\'s two-year proposal and Kastor\'s one-year proposal fall within the enforceability window. The compromise position of 18 months sits squarely in the safe harbor recognized by Delaware courts and would be virtually unassailable on temporal-reasonableness grounds. Indeed, it would be difficult for a challenging party to articulate a credible argument that 18 months is unreasonably long in the context of a specialty polymer coatings joint venture involving substantial technology transfer, customer relationship development, and proprietary formulation know-how.

**B. Texas.** Texas enforces **one- to two-year** covenants not to compete in commercial and sale-of-business contexts, subject to the requirements of the Texas Covenants Not to Compete Act (the \"Texas Act\"). The Texas Act provides both the enforceability standard and a mandatory reformation mechanism that makes Texas one of the most covenant-friendly jurisdictions in the country.

**Key Statutory Provisions.**

Section 15.50 of the Texas Business and Commerce Code provides that a covenant not to compete is enforceable if it: (a) is \"ancillary to or part of an otherwise enforceable agreement\"; and (b) contains \"limitations as to time, geographical area, and scope of activity to be restrained that are reasonable and do not impose a greater restraint than is necessary to protect the goodwill or other business interest of the promisee.\" This two-pronged test --- the \"ancillary\" requirement and the \"reasonableness\" requirement --- is well established and has been consistently applied by Texas courts.

Section 15.51(a) provides the reformation mechanism: if a court finds that a covenant not to compete is unreasonable as to its limitations, the court \"shall reform the covenant to the extent necessary to cause the limitations contained in the covenant as to time, geographical area, and scope of activity to be restrained to be reasonable and enforce the covenant as reformed.\" This mandatory reformation provision --- discussed further in Section VI below --- effectively guarantees that a covenant will be enforced in some form, even if the original terms are found overbroad, provided that the covenant is ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement.

Section 15.52 provides a specific exemption for covenants ancillary to the sale of a business. Such covenants are \"enforceable if the restraint is limited in time, geographical area, and scope of activity to be restrained and bears a reasonable relationship to the value of the business.\" This provision confirms that sale-of-business non-competes, including those arising in the JV exit context, receive more deferential treatment under Texas law than do employment non-competes.

**Key Case Authority.**

In *Marsh USA Inc. v. Cook*, 354 S.W.3d 764 (Tex. 2012), the Texas Supreme Court reaffirmed that sale-of-business non-competes receive more deferential review than employment non-competes under the Texas Act. The court observed that the parties to a business sale are sophisticated commercial actors negotiating at arm\'s length, and that the consideration paid for the business (or, in the JV context, the value of the membership interest) provides ample support for the restrictive covenant. The court noted that two-year restraints have been \"consistently upheld\" in sale-of-business cases and that Texas courts should be reluctant to second-guess the commercial judgment of parties who have agreed to such terms as part of a negotiated transaction.

In *Peat Marwick Main & Co. v. Haass*, the Texas Court of Appeals upheld an 18-month non-competition covenant, characterizing the duration as a \"moderate\" restraint reflecting the typical period needed to dissipate the goodwill transference that occurs when one party exits a business arrangement. The court noted that 18 months was well within the range of reasonableness and that the departing party had failed to demonstrate any undue hardship resulting from the restraint period.

**Application to MKPoly.** Because MKPoly will be headquartered and operationally based in Houston, Texas, the Texas enforcement standards are directly relevant to any enforcement action brought in the state where the JV conducts its day-to-day business. The 18-month compromise sits at the center of Texas\'s recognized safe harbor for sale-of-business non-competes. Even Meridian\'s full two-year proposal would likely survive challenge under the deferential review applicable to covenants governed by Section 15.52. Texas\'s mandatory reformation statute (§ 15.51) provides an additional safety net: even if a court were to conclude that two years is slightly longer than necessary, the court would be required to reform the covenant to a reasonable duration and enforce it as reformed, rather than voiding it entirely. This safety net, while valuable, should not be viewed as a substitute for drafting within the safe harbor, because Section 15.51(c) permits the promisor to recover attorneys\' fees if the original covenant is found to be unreasonable --- a point we address further in Section VI below.

**C. New York**

New York courts apply a well-developed reasonableness framework to commercial and sale-of-business non-competes. In this context, New York is notably more permissive than Delaware or Texas: courts routinely enforce **two- to three-year** post-closing restraints and have upheld restraints of **up to five years** where justified by the value of the transferred goodwill and the nature of the business.

**Key Case Authority.**

In *BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg*, 93 N.Y.2d 382 (1999), the New York Court of Appeals articulated the three-part reasonableness test applicable to non-competition covenants: the covenant is enforceable only if it (i) is not greater than required for the protection of the legitimate interest of the promisee, (ii) does not impose undue hardship on the promisor, and (iii) is not injurious to the public. The court emphasized that a \"more permissive standard\" applies to sale-of-business covenants than to employment restraints, recognizing that the seller of a business \"presumably receive\[s\] substantial consideration for \[the\] promise not to compete\" and is in a fundamentally different bargaining position than an employee.

In *Purchasing Associates, Inc. v. Weitz*, 13 N.Y.2d 267 (1963), the Court of Appeals upheld a **five-year** non-competition restraint ancillary to the sale of a business. The court observed that the length of the restraint was proportionate to the substantial goodwill transferred, the breadth of the seller\'s customer relationships, and the difficulty the buyer would face in establishing independent goodwill within a shorter period. The court noted that in the sale-of-business context, \"the parties are in the best position to assess the value of the goodwill sold and the period of time necessary to protect that investment.\"

**Application to MKPoly.** New York would enforce any duration between one and two years without difficulty; indeed, even a three-year restraint would likely survive challenge in the sale-of-business context. The 18-month compromise is well within the permissible range under New York law and would face no meaningful challenge on temporal-reasonableness grounds. New York law is relevant if the parties choose New York as the governing law (a possible Kastor request given Rennert Krieger Solano LLP\'s New York base) or if enforcement is sought against Kastor personnel with New York contacts. In either scenario, New York\'s standards would not constrain the negotiated compromise.

**D. California.** California\'s approach to non-competition covenants is fundamentally different from the other surveyed jurisdictions and requires careful treatment.

**General Statutory Prohibition.** California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 provides, in unambiguous terms, that \"every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.\" This provision has been interpreted by the California Supreme Court as establishing a near-absolute prohibition on non-competition covenants, subject only to the narrow statutory exceptions codified in Sections 16601 through 16602.5.

**LLC/Partnership Exception.** Section 16602.5, which was enacted to address the specific context of LLC member dissociation, provides that a member of a limited liability company may, upon the dissolution of the LLC or the dissociation of the member, agree not to carry on a similar business within a specified geographic area where the LLC carries on business, \"so long as any other member of the LLC, or a person deriving title to the business or goodwill of the LLC, continues to carry on a like business therein.\" This exception parallels the partnership dissolution exception in Section 16602 and is intended to protect the continuing members\' investment in the LLC\'s goodwill.

**Key Case Authority.**

In *Strategix, Ltd. v. Infocrossing West, Inc.*, the court discussed the narrow scope of the LLC exception under Section 16602.5, emphasizing that the exception applies only when the covenant is tied to a genuine dissolution or dissociation event and the geographic and activity restrictions correspond to the LLC\'s actual business operations. The court declined to extend the exception to a covenant that was, in substance, an employment restraint dressed up as an LLC exit provision.

In *Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP*, 44 Cal. 4th 937 (2008), the California Supreme Court delivered a landmark decision broadly reading Section 16600 as prohibiting all non-competition covenants except those falling squarely within the statutory exceptions. The court expressly rejected the common-law \"narrow restraint\" doctrine --- previously recognized by some California appellate courts --- under which a narrowly tailored restraint could be enforced even if it did not fall within a statutory exception. After *Edwards*, the statutory exceptions represent the exclusive basis for enforcing a non-competition covenant under California law.

**Application to MKPoly.** If Kastor (or a Kastor affiliate such as Kastor Specialty Products, Inc.) has California business operations, or if MKPoly ever sells its products in California, enforcement of the non-compete against Kastor in a California forum presents material risk. To maximize enforceability under the Section 16602.5 exception, the covenant should be structured to satisfy the following requirements:

> \(a\) The covenant must be expressly ancillary to the exit, dissolution, or dissociation of a member\'s interest in MKPoly --- not characterized as an employment-related restraint or a general commercial restriction.
>
> \(b\) The geographic scope should be limited to areas where MKPoly actually conducted business, consistent with the statutory language requiring the restriction to correspond to \"a specified geographic area where the \[LLC\] carries on business.\"
>
> \(c\) The duration should be moderate. While Section 16602.5 does not specify a maximum duration, California courts are likely to view a shorter restraint period more favorably in light of the state\'s strong public policy against restraints on trade.

The 18-month compromise is defensible under the Section 16602.5 exception but should be paired with a narrow geographic and product scope to maximize the likelihood that the covenant fits within the statutory safe harbor. Critically, a **forum-selection clause designating Delaware or Texas** for enforcement of Section 11.01 significantly mitigates California risk, because it reduces the likelihood that a California court will be called upon to apply Section 16600. However, as discussed in Section VIII.C below, a California court could decline to enforce the forum-selection clause if it determines that California\'s public policy against non-competes constitutes a \"fundamental\" public policy sufficient to override the contractual choice of forum.

**[IV. Geographic Scope Limitations]{.underline}**

The geographic scope of the non-competition covenant is the second dimension of reasonableness and the dimension on which the parties\' current positions are most readily amenable to compromise. Meridian proposes \"all of North America.\" Kastor proposes \"only territories where MKPoly had active sales at exit.\" Below, we survey the permissible geographic scope in each jurisdiction and recommend a hybrid formulation.

**A. Delaware.** Delaware courts take a **fact-specific approach** to geographic scope, evaluating whether the territorial restriction corresponds to the areas in which the business actually operates, sells, or has established goodwill. The inquiry is pragmatic rather than formulaic: a geographic restriction is reasonable if it covers the territory in which the restrained party could meaningfully compete with the business from which it is departing.

In *Weichert Co. of Pa. v. Young* and analogous Delaware Chancery decisions, the court held that geographic scope should correspond to the \"actual market reach\" of the restrained party\'s prior activities within the business. Where a business operates across a broad territory --- including nationally or internationally --- a correspondingly broad geographic restriction is permissible. Conversely, where a business is purely local, a nationwide restriction would be viewed as overbroad and potentially unenforceable.

**Application to MKPoly.** Meridian proposes a \"North America\" restriction. Kastor proposes that the geographic scope be limited to \"only territories where MKPoly had active sales at exit.\" If MKPoly\'s operational launch --- currently targeted for Q1 2026 --- results in active sales across the United States and Canada (as projected in the JV\'s initial business plan, attached as Exhibit B to the JVA), a North American scope should be enforceable under Delaware law. However, the enforceability analysis is strongest where the territorial scope is clearly tethered to the JV\'s actual market presence rather than defined by arbitrary political boundaries.

Kastor\'s narrower formulation --- limiting the restriction to territories of actual sales --- reduces litigation risk and may be acceptable to Meridian if paired with a reasonably broad product scope. However, this formulation introduces definitional ambiguity: what constitutes an \"active sale\" in a given \"territory\"? Does a single shipment to a customer in a jurisdiction constitute an \"active sale\" in that \"territory\"? If territories are defined by state or province, Kastor could arguably compete freely in any state where MKPoly had only incidental sales.

We recommend a **hybrid formulation** that addresses both parties\' concerns: the restricted territory should be defined as **the United States, Canada, and any other jurisdiction where MKPoly generated revenues exceeding a de minimis threshold (e.g., \$500,000 in the aggregate) during the twenty-four (24) months preceding the applicable exit event**. This formulation provides the following advantages:

> \(i\) It is anchored to MKPoly\'s actual commercial presence, satisfying Delaware\'s nexus requirement.
>
> \(ii\) The revenue threshold eliminates jurisdictions where MKPoly had only incidental or de minimis sales, addressing Kastor\'s concern about overbreadth.
>
> \(iii\) The use of a defined lookback period (24 months) provides certainty and avoids disputes about whether MKPoly was \"active\" in a given territory at an arbitrary point in time.
>
> \(iv\) If MKPoly is, as expected, active throughout the United States and Canada by the time any exit event occurs, the practical effect of this formulation is essentially identical to Meridian\'s \"North America\" proposal --- but with a defensible analytical foundation.

**B. Texas.** The Texas statutory standard requires that a geographic restriction bear a \"reasonable relationship to the value of the business\" (Section 15.52). Texas courts have upheld **nationwide and multi-state restrictions** where supported by evidence that the business\'s customer relationships, sales activities, and goodwill extend across the restrained territory.

In *Zep Inc. v. Zinser* and similar Texas appellate decisions, courts have enforced geographic restrictions covering the entire United States where the business in question had a national sales force, national distribution network, and national customer base. The courts have reasoned that a national business generates national goodwill, and that a geographic restriction coextensive with that goodwill is reasonable under Section 15.52.

**Application to MKPoly.** Houston-based operations with national and continental distribution would support a North American geographic scope under Texas law. The recommended hybrid formulation --- tied to a revenue threshold --- is fully consistent with the Texas statutory standard and would satisfy the \"reasonable relationship to the value of the business\" requirement without difficulty.

**C. New York**

New York takes a **flexible approach** to geographic scope in the sale-of-business context. Courts have upheld nationwide and even international geographic restrictions where the business operated nationally or globally and the departing party had knowledge of and relationships with customers across the restrained territory.

In *Brown & Brown, Inc. v. Johnson* and similar decisions, New York courts have enforced broad geographic restrictions without requiring the promisee to demonstrate, territory by territory, that the restrained party had personal contacts in every jurisdiction covered by the restriction. The courts have reasoned that in the sale-of-business context, the seller\'s knowledge of the business\'s operations, customers, pricing, and strategy creates a competitive threat that is not confined to the specific locations where the seller personally conducted business.

**Application to MKPoly.** New York\'s flexible standard would accommodate either Meridian\'s \"North America\" proposal or the recommended hybrid formulation. Geographic scope is unlikely to be a point of vulnerability under New York law.

**D. California.** Section 16602.5 requires that the geographic restriction correspond to the area \"where the \[LLC\] carries on business.\" This is the **most constraining standard** of the four surveyed jurisdictions and provides the strongest support for Kastor\'s position that the territory should be limited to areas of actual JV operations and sales.

Importantly, the statutory language does not permit a geographic restriction based on areas where the LLC *could* or *might* carry on business; the restriction must correspond to areas where the LLC actually does carry on business. This requirement reflects California\'s strong public policy against restraints on trade and its intent to confine the LLC exception to the narrowest scope consistent with protecting the continuing members\' goodwill.

**Practical Recommendation.** If the non-compete is to be enforceable in a California forum --- even as a fallback position --- the geographic scope must be tied to MKPoly\'s actual market footprint. The recommended hybrid formulation (United States, Canada, and jurisdictions meeting a revenue threshold) satisfies this requirement, provided that MKPoly in fact generates revenues in those jurisdictions. This alignment with Kastor\'s concern about overbreadth does not materially weaken Meridian\'s protection if MKPoly is, as expected, active throughout North America. In practice, the hybrid formulation is the best available compromise: it is broad enough to protect Meridian\'s commercial interests and narrow enough to satisfy California\'s statutory nexus requirement.

**[V. Product-Scope Reasonableness Standards]{.underline}**

The product or activity scope of the non-competition covenant determines the range of business activities from which the departing member is restrained. This is the dimension on which the parties\' positions present the greatest potential for creative compromise, and on which the risk of overbreadth is most acute.

**A. Delaware.** Delaware courts require that the activity restraint be **\"no broader than necessary\"** to protect the promisee\'s legitimate interest. In the sale-of-business and JV exit context, the scope of the restrained activity should correspond to the business of the JV as actually conducted, not to the entirety of either party\'s corporate activities across all product lines and markets.

The Court of Chancery has consistently held that a non-compete covering products or services that the transferred business never developed, manufactured, or sold is overbroad and may be voided or reformed. The rationale is straightforward: the promisee\'s protectable interest --- the goodwill acquired through the transaction --- does not extend to product categories in which the business had no presence and generated no goodwill.

**Analysis of the Parties\' Positions.**

*Meridian\'s proposed scope --- \"all specialty polymer coatings.\"* This formulation is problematic under Delaware law because the term \"specialty polymer coatings\" is a broad industry category that encompasses numerous product lines, applications, and end markets that MKPoly may never have developed, manufactured, or sold. If, for example, MKPoly focuses on automotive OEM coatings and industrial protective coatings but never enters the aerospace coatings or marine coatings segments, a restraint covering \"all specialty polymer coatings\" would extend to product categories unrelated to the JV\'s actual business and goodwill. Delaware courts would likely view this formulation as overbroad and could decline to enforce it --- or, at minimum, reform it to a narrower scope.

*Kastor\'s proposed scope --- \"only specific JV product lines at exit.\"* This formulation addresses the overbreadth concern but introduces a different risk: it is potentially too narrow and could invite gamesmanship. If the restrained activity is defined by a fixed list of specific product names or SKUs as of the exit date, Kastor could pivot immediately to closely adjacent products that are functionally identical or directly competitive with MKPoly\'s products but technically differ from the listed product lines in formulation, branding, or application. This would effectively nullify the non-compete by allowing Kastor to replicate MKPoly\'s competitive position without technically violating the covenant.

*Recommended compromise.* We recommend defining the restricted activity by reference to:

> \(i\) the products or product categories actually produced, marketed, or under active development by MKPoly as of the applicable exit date; **plus**
>
> \(ii\) products that are substantially similar to, or directly competitive with, such products.

This formulation tracks the JV\'s Business Purpose as set forth in Schedule 2.04 of the MKPoly JVA while providing a protective \"penumbra\" that prevents the departing member from exploiting technical product distinctions to circumvent the covenant\'s intent. The \"substantially similar or directly competitive\" language is a standard formulation in commercial non-competes and has been upheld by Delaware courts as a reasonable extension of the promisee\'s protectable interest.

**B. Texas.** Texas courts apply a **\"reasonable relationship\" test** under Section 15.52: the restrained activity must \"bear a reasonable relationship to the value of the business\" being sold or from which the member is departing. This standard is functionally equivalent to Delaware\'s \"no broader than necessary\" formulation and yields the same result: the restrained activity must correspond to the JV\'s actual business, not to the parties\' broader corporate activities.

Texas appellate courts have upheld non-competes that define the restrained activity by reference to the product categories of the transferred business plus \"substantially similar\" or \"directly competitive\" products. This language has been treated as a reasonable description of the scope of the promisee\'s protectable interest.

The recommended compromise formulation --- products actually produced, marketed, or under active development by MKPoly, plus substantially similar or directly competitive products --- is fully consistent with the Texas statutory standard.

**C. New York**

New York courts generally permit **broader activity restrictions** in the sale-of-business context than in the employment context but will strike or reform restrictions that prevent the promisor from engaging in businesses unrelated to the one sold. The Court of Appeals in *Purchasing Associates, Inc. v. Weitz* upheld a broad activity restriction that covered the seller\'s \"field\" of business activity, and in *Reed, Roberts Associates, Inc. v. Strauman*, 40 N.Y.2d 303 (1976), the court confirmed that the restrained activity must bear a reasonable nexus to the business sold but need not be limited to the precise products or services offered at the time of sale.

Under New York law, Meridian\'s \"all specialty polymer coatings\" formulation would have a better chance of enforcement than under Delaware or Texas law, given New York\'s tolerance for broader scope in the sale-of-business context. However, even New York courts would be likely to narrow the scope if the evidence showed that MKPoly\'s actual business covered only a subset of the specialty polymer coatings market. The recommended compromise formulation is therefore advisable under New York law as well, both for enforceability and for consistency across jurisdictions.

**D. California.** Under Section 16602.5, the restriction must be on **\"a similar business.\"** This standard requires a close nexus between the restricted activity and MKPoly\'s actual business. A restraint covering \"all specialty polymer coatings\" would almost certainly be viewed as extending beyond \"a similar business\" if MKPoly\'s actual product line is narrower than the full universe of specialty polymer coatings.

The recommended compromise formulation --- tied to the JV\'s actual and substantially similar products --- satisfies the \"similar business\" requirement under Section 16602.5 and represents the strongest available position for enforcement in a California forum.

**[VI. Blue-Pencil and Reformation Doctrines]{.underline}**

The reformation and blue-pencil doctrines determine whether a court will modify an overbroad covenant to make it enforceable or will instead void the covenant in its entirety. These doctrines vary significantly across the four surveyed jurisdictions and have important implications both for drafting strategy and for litigation risk.

**A. Delaware.** Delaware applies a **\"reasonable modification\" approach**: the Court of Chancery may modify an overbroad covenant to make it reasonable and then enforce it as modified. However, the court retains discretion to decline modification if the covenant is so overreaching as to suggest that the drafter acted in bad faith --- i.e., that the drafter intentionally included overbroad terms in the expectation that a court would narrow the covenant to a reasonable scope, thereby achieving a broader restraint than the drafter could have obtained through good-faith negotiation.

In *FP UC Holdings, LLC v. Hamilton* and other Chancery decisions, the court has identified the following factors relevant to the exercise of its reformation discretion: (i) the degree of overbreadth (a covenant that is modestly overbroad is more likely to be reformed than one that is grossly overreaching); (ii) whether the covenant was the product of arm\'s-length negotiation between sophisticated parties; (iii) whether the overbreadth appears intentional or inadvertent; and (iv) whether the promisor would be unduly prejudiced by enforcement of the reformed covenant.

**Practical Implication.** Drafting the non-compete to be facially reasonable --- within the safe-harbor parameters identified in this memorandum --- is strongly preferable to relying on judicial reformation. Delaware reformation is a discretionary remedy, not a guaranteed one, and a court that concludes the drafter acted in bad faith may void the covenant entirely. The recommended compromise formulation is designed to fall within the safe harbor on all three dimensions of reasonableness, minimizing the need to invoke reformation as a fallback.

**B. Texas.** Texas\'s mandatory reformation statute --- Section 15.51(a) of the Business and Commerce Code --- provides the **most covenant-friendly reformation regime** among the four surveyed jurisdictions. Unlike Delaware\'s discretionary approach, Texas law requires the court to reform an overbroad covenant and enforce the reformed version. The statute leaves no discretion: if the court finds that the limitations are unreasonable, the court \"shall reform the covenant to the extent necessary to cause the limitations ... to be reasonable\" and \"shall enforce the covenant as reformed.\"

This mandatory reformation provision effectively eliminates the risk that an overbroad covenant will be voided entirely under Texas law. However, it does not eliminate all risk. Section 15.51(c) provides that if the court finds the original covenant unreasonable, the promisor \"may be awarded court costs and reasonable attorney\'s fees.\" This fee-shifting provision creates a practical incentive to draft within the safe harbor: even if the covenant will ultimately be enforced (as reformed), the promisee may be required to pay the promisor\'s litigation costs if the original covenant is found to have been unreasonable. In a complex commercial dispute, these costs can be substantial.

**Practical Implication.** Texas\'s mandatory reformation statute provides a strong safety net, but Meridian should not treat it as a license to draft an overbroad covenant. The risk of adverse fee-shifting, combined with the reputational cost of having a covenant reformed by a court, counsels in favor of drafting within the safe-harbor parameters from the outset.

**C. New York**

New York applies a **partial enforcement / blue-pencil doctrine**: courts will sever or narrow an overbroad restraint to the extent needed to make it reasonable, provided the covenant was not drafted in bad faith. The Court of Appeals in *BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg* expressly endorsed this approach, observing that \"the modern, prevailing common-law standard of reasonableness\" permits a court to \"sever the unenforceable portion of the restrictive covenant and enforce the remainder.\"

New York courts are generally willing to reform duration, geography, and scope independently, narrowing whichever dimension is overbroad while leaving the remaining dimensions intact. This independent-reformation approach is more flexible than a strict blue-pencil doctrine (which would permit only the deletion of overbroad clauses, not their modification) and provides substantial protection for the promisee even if one dimension of the covenant is found to be overbroad.

**Practical Implication.** New York\'s reformation doctrine is a useful fallback, but, as in Delaware, the court retains discretion to decline reformation if the covenant was drafted in bad faith. The recommended compromise formulation is designed to be reasonable on all three dimensions, reducing the need to invoke reformation.

**D. California.** California **does not apply the blue-pencil doctrine** to non-competition covenants that fall outside the statutory exceptions (Sections 16601--16602.5). If a non-compete does not qualify under the LLC/partnership exception or another statutory exception, it is **void in its entirety** --- a California court will not narrow it to save it. The California Supreme Court\'s decision in *Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP* made this point emphatically, holding that Section 16600 \"protects Californians and ensures \'that every citizen shall retain the right to pursue any lawful employment and enterprise of their choice,\'\" and that courts may not circumvent this protection by reforming an otherwise void covenant into one that falls within a statutory exception.

This all-or-nothing approach makes California the most hostile jurisdiction for non-competition covenants and underscores the critical importance of ensuring that the covenant, as drafted, satisfies the Section 16602.5 requirements on its face rather than relying on judicial rescue.

**Practical Recommendation.** We recommend including a **severability and reformation clause** in Section 11.01 of the MKPoly JVA that expressly authorizes a court of competent jurisdiction to reform the covenant to the minimum extent necessary to render it enforceable under applicable law. While this clause will not be effective in a California court (which would disregard it under *Edwards*), it will be effective in Delaware, Texas, and New York --- the jurisdictions most likely to adjudicate enforceability.

To mitigate the California risk, the severability and reformation clause should be paired with a **forum-selection clause** directing enforcement actions under Section 11.01 to the Delaware Court of Chancery or, in the alternative, the federal or state courts of Harris County, Texas. This forum-selection clause will channel enforcement litigation to jurisdictions where reformation is available and where the covenant is most likely to be enforced as drafted. We discuss this recommendation further in Section VIII.B below.
